In enterprise environments, security tools like Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) or Network Performance Monitors need to see all traffic on a switch. Administrators configure a Switch Port Analyzer (SPAN) port, also known as a mirror port. The switch is instructed to copy all traffic traversing specific ports (or entire VLANs) and send that copy to the SPAN port where the IDS is listening. If an attacker gains administrative access to a switch, they can configure a SPAN port to forward all traffic directly to their own machine for analysis.

topics.title: Network Traffic Analysis Tools
topics.content:
Analyzing captured network traffic requires specialized tools capable of dissecting complex network protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS, SMB) down to the byte level.

Wireshark

Wireshark is the undisputed industry standard for graphical network protocol analysis. It can capture live traffic from a network interface or read offline captures in the standard PCAP (Packet Capture) format. The true power of Wireshark lies in its extensive protocol decoders and its incredibly powerful Display Filters. Display filters allow analysts to sift through millions of packets to find exactly what they need.

Examples of Wireshark Display Filters:

  • tcp.port == 80: Shows only HTTP web traffic.
  • ip.src == 192.168.1.50 && ip.dst == 10.0.0.5: Shows traffic originating from a specific source going to a specific destination.
  • http.request.method == "POST": Filters for HTTP POST requests, which are often used to submit login credentials or exfiltrate data.
  • ftp.request.command == "USER": Isolates FTP login attempts.

tcpdump

tcpdump is a lightweight, ubiquitous command-line packet sniffer found on almost all Unix and Linux systems. It is preferred for running long-term captures on headless servers or firewalls where a GUI is unavailable. Because it is highly optimized, it can handle massive traffic volumes without dropping packets.

Unlike Wireshark's display filters (which filter data after it is captured), tcpdump uses Capture Filters based on the Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF) syntax. Capture filters drop unwanted packets at the kernel level before they are even processed or saved to disk, drastically reducing resource usage.

Examples of tcpdump commands:

  • tcpdump -i eth0 -n tcp port 80: Captures traffic on the eth0 interface, disables DNS resolution (-n for speed), and captures only TCP port 80 traffic.
  • tcpdump -i any -w capture.pcap src 192.168.1.100: Captures traffic from any interface originating from a specific IP and writes it to a file (-w) for later analysis in Wireshark.

topics.title: MAC Attacks and Switch Exploitation
topics.content:
Attackers target the fundamental operational mechanics of Layer 2 switches to perform active sniffing.

CAM Table Exhaustion (MAC Flooding)

A switch relies on a Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table (also known as a MAC address table) to map physical MAC addresses to physical switch ports. This table has a finite size (e.g., 8,000 entries). A MAC Flooding attack exploits this limitation. The attacker uses a tool like macof to rapidly generate thousands of Ethernet frames per second, each with a randomly spoofed, fake source MAC address. The switch attempts to learn all these fake addresses, rapidly filling the CAM table to capacity.

When the CAM table is full, the switch enters a state called "fail-open." Unable to process new MAC addresses and uncertain where to send legitimate incoming traffic, the switch reverts to behaving exactly like a hub: it broadcasts all incoming frames out of every port. The attacker, sitting on one of those ports in promiscuous mode, can now passively sniff all traffic on the VLAN.

VLAN Hopping

Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs) logically separate broadcast domains on a switch for security and performance. Attackers use VLAN hopping to bypass these logical boundaries and access traffic on other VLANs.

  • Switch Spoofing: Attackers exploit dynamic trunking protocols (like Cisco's DTP). If a switch port is configured to automatically negotiate a trunk (a link that carries traffic for all VLANs), the attacker connects a machine configured to act like a switch. The port negotiates a trunk link, granting the attacker access to traffic across all configured VLANs.
  • Double Tagging: The attacker crafts a malicious frame containing two 802.1Q VLAN tags. The first switch processes and strips the outer tag (which belongs to the native VLAN), forwarding the frame to the next switch. The second switch processes the inner, hidden tag and forwards the packet to the victim's secure VLAN. This is a one-way attack, but highly effective for delivering exploits across VLAN boundaries.

topics.title: ARP, DHCP, and DNS Spoofing
topics.content:
These attacks exploit the implicit trust inherent in core local network protocols to establish a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) position.

ARP Spoofing (ARP Poisoning)

The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) resolves logical IP addresses to physical MAC addresses. ARP is a stateless, unauthenticated protocol. Any device can send an ARP reply, even if an ARP request was never made. In an ARP Spoofing attack, the attacker sends continuous, forged ARP reply packets to a victim machine (e.g., a workstation) and the network's default gateway (the router).

The forged packets tell the victim: "The MAC address of the default gateway is [Attacker's MAC]". Simultaneously, they tell the gateway: "The MAC address of the victim is [Attacker's MAC]". Both devices update their ARP caches with the malicious data. Now, all traffic between the victim and the internet flows directly through the attacker's machine, allowing them to sniff, modify, or drop the packets. Tools like Ettercap and Bettercap automate this attack effortlessly.

DHCP Starvation and Rogue DHCP

The Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) automatically assigns IP addresses and network configurations (like the default gateway and DNS server) to clients. DHCP Starvation involves an attacker broadcasting thousands of DHCP DISCOVER requests, each with a spoofed source MAC address. The legitimate DHCP server leases out an IP address for every fake request until its entire IP pool is exhausted.

With the legitimate server unable to service new clients, the attacker launches a Rogue DHCP Server. When new devices connect to the network and request an IP, the attacker's rogue server responds first. It provides valid IP addresses but maliciously configures the client's default gateway or DNS server to point directly to the attacker's machine, establishing a MitM attack for the entire network segment.

DNS Spoofing (DNS Poisoning)

DNS spoofing involves intercepting legitimate DNS requests (e.g., a user trying to visit www.bank.com) and replying with a forged IP address. The victim's browser is transparently redirected to an attacker-controlled web server designed to look identical to the legitimate site, allowing the attacker to harvest login credentials. This can be achieved via local ARP spoofing (intercepting the DNS queries locally) or by compromising a DNS server directly.

topics.title: Sniffing Countermeasures
topics.content:
Defending against sniffing and Layer 2 attacks requires robust configuration of network switches and the implementation of strong encryption protocols.

Layer 2 Mitigations (Switch Security)

  • Port Security: Directly mitigates MAC flooding (CAM table exhaustion). Administrators configure the switch port to allow only a specific number of MAC addresses (e.g., maximum of 2). If the port detects a flood of fake MACs exceeding the threshold, it automatically shuts down (err-disable state) or drops the offending traffic, stopping the attack instantly.
  • Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI): Directly mitigates ARP Spoofing. DAI relies on a trusted database of IP-to-MAC bindings (usually generated by DHCP Snooping). The switch intercepts all ARP packets on untrusted ports, inspects them, and silently discards any ARP replies where the IP and MAC addresses do not match the trusted database.
  • DHCP Snooping: Mitigates Rogue DHCP servers and DHCP starvation. The switch administrator configures the port connected to the legitimate corporate DHCP server as "Trusted." All other ports (where users plug in) are "Untrusted." The switch will immediately drop any DHCP server messages (like DHCP OFFER or ACK) originating from untrusted ports, neutralizing rogue servers. It also rate-limits DHCP requests to prevent starvation attacks.

Encryption and Protocol Security

The ultimate defense against packet sniffing is ensuring the attacker cannot read the data even if they successfully capture it. Network administrators must strictly enforce the use of encrypted protocols:

  • Replace Telnet with SSH for remote administration.
  • Replace FTP with SFTP or FTPS.
  • Enforce HTTPS (TLS/SSL) for all web traffic using technologies like HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS).
  • Utilize IPsec VPNs for secure communication between network segments or remote workers.
  • Implement IPv6 securely. While IPv6 eliminates traditional ARP, it introduces Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP), which is vulnerable to similar spoofing attacks (NDP Spoofing). Security features like RA Guard (Router Advertisement Guard) must be deployed.
