modules.title: Hacking Wireless Networks
modules.description: Comprehensive textbook-level coverage of wireless networking standards, encryption protocols, and advanced wireless attack methodologies including evil twin attacks, KRACK, Bluetooth exploitation, and RFID cloning.
objectives: Understand the intricate details of wireless standards (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax) and encryption (WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3).
Execute and analyze advanced wireless attacks such as Evil Twin, KRACK, and deauthentication.
Analyze Bluetooth and RFID security flaws, including Bluejacking, Bluebugging, and RFID cloning.
Utilize enterprise-grade wireless auditing tools like Aircrack-ng suite, Kismet, and Reaver.

topics.title: Wireless Standards and Encryption
topics.content:
802.11 Wireless Standards

The IEEE 802.11 standard family governs wireless local area networks (WLANs). Understanding these standards is critical for a penetration tester to determine the capabilities, frequencies, and vulnerabilities of a target network.

  • 802.11a/b/g: Legacy standards. 802.11b/g operate in the 2.4 GHz band, which is heavily congested but offers better penetration through solid objects. 802.11a operates in the 5 GHz band, offering higher bandwidth but shorter range.
  • 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4): Introduced Multiple Input Multiple Output (MIMO) technology, allowing multiple antennas to transmit and receive data simultaneously, significantly increasing throughput and range. Operates on both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands.
  • 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5): Operates exclusively in the 5 GHz band, supporting wider channels (up to 160 MHz) and Multi-User MIMO (MU-MIMO) for downlink traffic, greatly enhancing speeds in dense environments.
  • 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6): Focuses on high efficiency. It introduces Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiple Access (OFDMA), allowing a single channel to be subdivided into smaller sub-channels for multiple users, reducing latency. It also supports Target Wake Time (TWT) for IoT devices.

Wireless Encryption Protocols

Securing wireless networks has evolved significantly over the years, moving from deeply flawed implementations to robust cryptographic standards.

  • WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy): The original 802.11 encryption standard, WEP is catastrophically flawed. It uses the RC4 stream cipher with a short 24-bit Initialization Vector (IV). Because the IV space is so small (16.7 million combinations), under heavy traffic, IVs are rapidly reused. Attackers capture these reused IVs to statistically derive the static WEP key in minutes using tools like aircrack-ng. WEP should never be used.
  • WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access): Designed as an interim replacement for WEP that could run on legacy hardware. It introduced TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol), which implemented per-packet key mixing and a message integrity check (Michael algorithm). While better than WEP, TKIP is still based on RC4 and is vulnerable to attacks like ChopChop and MIC exploitation.
  • WPA2 (Wi-Fi Protected Access II): The long-standing enterprise standard. WPA2 mandates the use of AES-CCMP (Advanced Encryption Standard in Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code Protocol). AES-CCMP provides robust data confidentiality and integrity. However, WPA2 relies on a 4-way handshake for key exchange, which is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks if the pre-shared key (PSK) is weak. It is also vulnerable to the KRACK attack.
  • WPA3 (Wi-Fi Protected Access III): The latest standard, WPA3 replaces the vulnerable PSK exchange with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), also known as the Dragonfly handshake. SAE is a zero-knowledge proof protocol that mandates forward secrecy and completely prevents offline dictionary attacks. WPA3 also introduces OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption) for public networks, encrypting traffic even on open hotspots.

topics.title: Wireless Attack Techniques and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
The WPA2 4-Way Handshake Attack

The most common attack against WPA2-Personal networks targets the 4-way handshake. When a legitimate client connects to an AP, they perform a 4-way handshake to establish a Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) derived from the Pre-Shared Key (PSK). By forcing a client to disconnect (via a deauthentication attack) and reconnect, an attacker can capture this handshake. Once captured, the attacker uses offline brute-force or dictionary attacks (using hashcat or aircrack-ng) to guess the PSK.

KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attacks)

KRACK is a fundamental flaw in the WPA2 protocol itself, discovered in 2017. It exploits the 4-way handshake by manipulating and replaying cryptographic handshake messages. The attacker tricks the victim into reinstalling an already-in-use encryption key. This resets the key's transmit nonce (packet number) and receive replay counter to zero. With nonce reuse, the attacker can decrypt traffic, replay packets, and in some configurations, forge or inject packets into the network. This affects all WPA2 implementations and requires vendor patching.

WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup) Exploitation

WPS was designed to easily pair devices using an 8-digit PIN. However, a catastrophic design flaw splits the PIN authentication into two halves (4 digits and 3 digits + 1 checksum). This reduces the brute-force space from 100,000,000 to just 11,000 combinations. Attackers use tools like Reaver or Bully to brute-force the WPS PIN, which ultimately reveals the WPA2 plaintext passphrase, rendering complex passwords useless. The mitigation is to completely disable WPS on the router.

Deauthentication and Disassociation Attacks

The 802.11 standard (prior to 802.11w Management Frame Protection) sends management frames (like deauthentication and disassociation) in plaintext and without authentication. An attacker can spoof the MAC address of the AP and send deauthentication frames to a client, forcing them to drop off the network. This is used for Denial of Service (DoS), to force a 4-way handshake capture, or to herd clients to an Evil Twin AP. Command example: aireplay-ng -0 10 -a [AP_MAC] -c [Client_MAC] wlan0mon.

topics.title: Evil Twin and Rogue Access Points
topics.content:
Rogue Access Points

A rogue AP is an unauthorized access point connected to a corporate network. Employees often bring in rogue APs for convenience, but they bypass enterprise security controls like 802.1X, providing an open door for attackers into the internal network. Detecting rogue APs requires continuous wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS) and physical site surveys.

Evil Twin Attacks

An Evil Twin is a malicious AP set up by an attacker to impersonate a legitimate network. The attacker configures the Evil Twin with the exact same SSID and often operates on a different channel with a stronger signal (using a high-gain Yagi or directional antenna). Clients automatically connect to the stronger signal.

Once connected, the attacker has a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) position. They can capture traffic, strip SSL/TLS using tools like sslstrip, or serve a captive portal. Captive Portal Phishing: The attacker presents a fake login page that mimics the corporate authentication portal or a firmware update page, tricking the user into entering their AD credentials or WPA2 passphrase.

Karma Attacks

A Karma attack exploits how clients probe for known networks. When a client's Wi-Fi is on, it actively sends Probe Requests containing SSIDs it has previously connected to (e.g., 'HomeNetwork', 'StarbucksWiFi'). A tool like the WiFi Pineapple listens to these requests and automatically spins up a rogue AP with the requested SSID, responding with a Probe Response. The client unknowingly connects to the attacker. Modern OSs mitigate this by using passive scanning or randomizing MAC addresses during probes, but Karma remains highly effective against legacy or misconfigured devices.

topics.title: Bluetooth and RFID Hacking
topics.content:
Bluetooth Security and Exploitation

Bluetooth is a ubiquitous WPAN (Wireless Personal Area Network) protocol. Its security model relies on pairing, which establishes a link key. Vulnerabilities often arise from weak pairing mechanisms or unpatched protocol flaws.

  • Bluejacking: The sending of unsolicited messages or business cards (vCards) over Bluetooth to discoverable devices. It is mostly a nuisance attack but demonstrates the device is in range and vulnerable.
  • Bluesnarfing: The unauthorized access of information from a wireless device through a Bluetooth connection, often between phones, desktops, and laptops. Attackers can steal contacts, emails, and text messages.
  • Bluebugging: A severe attack where the attacker gains full backdoor access to a Bluetooth device. The attacker can issue AT commands, allowing them to make phone calls, send SMS, and eavesdrop on conversations without the user's knowledge.
  • BlueBorne: A critical set of vulnerabilities discovered in 2017 affecting billions of devices. It allowed attackers to completely take over devices via Bluetooth without pairing or any user interaction, spreading over the air like a worm.

RFID and NFC Hacking

RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) and its subset NFC (Near Field Communication) are heavily used for physical access control (badges) and contactless payments.

  • RFID Cloning: Many legacy access badges (like 125 kHz HID Prox) transmit their unique ID in plaintext when energized by a reader. Attackers use tools like the Proxmark3 to read the badge from a victim's pocket, store the ID, and clone it onto a blank card or emulate it directly, bypassing physical security.
  • NFC Relay Attacks: Also known as a relay attack, an attacker uses two devices to bridge the NFC communication between a victim's smart card (or phone) and a legitimate reader over a distance, tricking the system into thinking the victim is physically present at the reader.

topics.title: Reconnaissance and Tooling
topics.content:
Wardriving

Wardriving is the act of physically moving around an area (typically in a vehicle) to map and log wireless networks. Using a GPS receiver, a high-gain antenna, and software like Kismet or NetStumbler, attackers can build comprehensive maps of vulnerable networks, plotting their locations on tools like WiGLE (Wireless Geographic Logging Engine).

The Aircrack-ng Suite

Aircrack-ng is the industry-standard suite for wireless network auditing. It comprises several tools:

  • airmon-ng: Places the wireless interface into monitor mode, allowing it to capture all traffic on a channel, not just traffic destined for its MAC address.
  • airodump-ng: A packet capture utility that captures 802.11 frames. It is used to identify target networks, find connected clients, and capture the WPA2 4-way handshake (saved as a .cap file).
  • aireplay-ng: Used for frame injection. It performs deauthentication attacks, ARP request replays, and ChopChop attacks to generate traffic and force handshakes.
  • aircrack-ng: The cracking component. It performs dictionary and brute-force attacks against the captured handshakes to recover WEP and WPA/WPA2 keys.

Passive Reconnaissance with Kismet

Kismet is a widely used 802.11 layer-2 wireless network detector, sniffer, and intrusion detection system (WIDS). Unlike active scanners that send probe requests, Kismet operates entirely passively. It listens to beacon frames and data traffic without transmitting anything, making it completely undetectable by target networks. It can identify hidden networks (non-broadcasting SSIDs) by analyzing traffic generated when a legitimate client connects to the hidden AP.

modules.title: Hacking Mobile Platforms
modules.description: In-depth exploration of mobile application security, Android and iOS architectures, dynamic instrumentation with Frida, MDM bypass, and advanced network evasion techniques.
objectives: Analyze Android and iOS security models, including sandboxing, code signing, and permissions.
Execute dynamic and static analysis of mobile applications to identify OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Understand the implications of rooting, jailbreaking, and MDM bypass in enterprise environments.
Apply advanced network evasion techniques including fragmentation, obfuscation, and firewalking to bypass IDS and Firewalls.

topics.title: Mobile Platform Architecture & Sandboxing
topics.content:
Android Security Architecture

Android is built on a customized Linux kernel, leveraging its robust permission and user isolation models. At the core of Android security is the Application Sandbox. Upon installation, every Android application is assigned a unique Linux User ID (UID). Because each app runs under a different UID, the Linux kernel enforces isolation: an app cannot read or write to another app's memory or storage space by default. The file system paths, such as /data/data/com.example.app/, are owned exclusively by that app's UID.

Inter-Process Communication (IPC) is tightly controlled. If apps need to share data, they must explicitly use Android IPC mechanisms like Intents, Content Providers, or bound Services, and declare the necessary permissions in the AndroidManifest.xml file. Furthermore, Android mandates APK Signing. Every application must be digitally signed by the developer. This ensures code integrity and verifies that subsequent updates come from the same author.

iOS Security Architecture

Apple's iOS employs a highly restrictive, multi-layered security model built on a customized Unix foundation (Darwin). The iOS architecture relies heavily on hardware integration, specifically the Secure Enclave, a dedicated coprocessor that handles cryptographic operations and biometric data processing independently from the main CPU.

The iOS Sandbox, enforced by the TrustedBSD mandatory access control framework (Seatbelt), severely restricts what an app can access. Unlike Android, apps cannot access the underlying file system outside their specific container directory. iOS also enforces strict Code Signing. Every application must be signed with an Apple-issued certificate. The kernel checks this signature before executing any code, effectively preventing the execution of dynamic, unsigned, or modified code, which is a massive barrier for malware and exploitation.

topics.title: Rooting, Jailbreaking, and MDM Bypass
topics.content:
Rooting and Jailbreaking Mechanics

Rooting (Android) and Jailbreaking (iOS) are processes aimed at bypassing manufacturer restrictions to attain superuser (root) privileges on the operating system. For Android, rooting typically involves unlocking the device's bootloader, flashing a custom recovery image, and installing a su binary and a management app like Magisk. Magisk uses a systemless approach, modifying the boot image rather than the system partition, allowing it to evade basic root detection mechanisms like SafetyNet.

Jailbreaking iOS is significantly more complex due to Apple's closed ecosystem. It involves chaining multiple exploits (e.g., WebKit vulnerabilities combined with kernel privilege escalation exploits) to disable code signing requirements and mount the root file system as read-write. Once jailbroken, tools like Cydia or Sileo are installed to manage unauthorized software.

Security Implications and MDM Bypass

From an enterprise security perspective, rooting and jailbreaking are catastrophic. They dismantle the application sandbox, allowing malicious applications to access sensitive data belonging to other apps, such as corporate emails, multi-factor authentication tokens, and VPN credentials. This completely invalidates the security posture of the device.

Mobile Device Management (MDM) solutions are deployed by enterprises to enforce policies, manage apps, and remote-wipe devices. However, sophisticated attackers or rogue employees can perform an MDM bypass. Once a device is rooted or jailbroken, MDM daemon processes can be killed, configuration profiles can be maliciously altered, and compliance checks can be spoofed. This allows a compromised device to remain on the corporate network while evading administrative oversight, presenting a massive data exfiltration risk.

topics.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10 & App Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
The OWASP Mobile Top 10

The OWASP Mobile Top 10 highlights the most critical security risks facing mobile applications. Penetration testers must focus heavily on these vectors.

  • M1: Improper Credential Usage: The most critical risk. This includes hardcoded API keys in the source code, insecure storage of credentials in SharedPreferences or SQLite databases without encryption, and improper implementation of biometric authentication frameworks. Attackers routinely decompile APKs to extract hardcoded AWS keys or backend credentials.
  • M2: Inadequate Supply Chain Security: Exploiting vulnerabilities in third-party libraries or SDKs integrated into the mobile app. If an SDK is compromised, the app itself inherits that vulnerability.
  • M3: Insecure Authentication/Authorization: Failing to properly validate the user's identity or permissions on the backend server. A mobile app might check authorization locally, which an attacker can bypass using tools like Frida, highlighting the rule: never trust the client.
  • M4: Insufficient Input/Output Validation: Leading to vulnerabilities like SQL Injection in local SQLite databases, or Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in mobile WebViews.
  • M5: Insecure Communication: Failing to properly secure network traffic. While TLS is widely used, apps often fail to implement Certificate Pinning. Without pinning, an attacker who installs a rogue CA certificate on the device can perform Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, intercepting and modifying API requests and responses.

Insecure Data Storage

Developers often mistakenly believe the mobile device itself provides adequate security. Storing sensitive PII, financial data, or session tokens in plain text in SharedPreferences, plist files, or external storage (SD card on Android) is a major flaw. If the device is rooted, or if the attacker gains physical access, this data is trivially extracted. Best practice mandates using Android Keystore or iOS Keychain for cryptographic key management and utilizing encrypted storage APIs.

topics.title: Mobile Attack Vectors & Dynamic Instrumentation
topics.content:
Static vs. Dynamic Analysis

Mobile application security testing involves two primary phases: static and dynamic analysis.

Static Analysis involves examining the application's source code or compiled binary without executing it. For Android, testers use tools like apktool to reverse engineer the APK, decode the AndroidManifest.xml, and extract resources. Jadx or dex2jar are used to decompile the Dalvik bytecode (DEX files) back into readable Java source code. Testers look for hardcoded secrets, misconfigured intents, and exported activities that shouldn't be public.

Dynamic Analysis involves running the application on a physical device or emulator and interacting with it. This includes intercepting network traffic using proxies like Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to identify API vulnerabilities.

Dynamic Instrumentation with Frida

Frida is the most powerful tool in modern mobile penetration testing. It is a dynamic instrumentation toolkit that allows researchers to inject JavaScript snippets or custom libraries into native apps on Android and iOS in real-time. With Frida, an attacker can:

  • Hook Functions: Intercept calls to specific methods, read their arguments, and modify their return values on the fly.
  • Bypass Defenses: If an app has root detection, Frida can hook the isDeviceRooted() method and force it to always return false.
  • Bypass Certificate Pinning: Frida scripts can hook the SSL context and TrustManager methods in Android to bypass certificate pinning, allowing MitM interception via Burp Suite even when the app aggressively validates certificates.
  • Extract Memory: Dump cryptographic keys or sensitive strings directly from the application's RAM during runtime.

Dynamic code loading is another attack vector. Malware often uses DexClassLoader on Android to dynamically download and execute malicious DEX files from a remote server after installation, bypassing initial app store static analysis.

topics.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
topics.content:
Understanding the Defense Perimeter

Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), such as Snort or Suricata, analyze network traffic for suspicious signatures and anomalies. Firewalls enforce access control lists (ACLs) based on ports and IPs. Honeypots are decoy systems designed to lure attackers. Evading these defenses is a critical skill for red teams.

Advanced Evasion Techniques

  • Packet Fragmentation: By breaking a malicious payload into tiny, overlapping IP fragments, an attacker can confuse an IDS. If the IDS engine processes fragments differently than the target operating system (e.g., overlapping fragment reassembly policies), the IDS might not recognize the signature, while the target reassembles and executes the attack.
  • Obfuscation and Encoding: Modifying the payload to hide its signature. Attackers use URL encoding (e.g., %2e%2e%2f for ../), hexadecimal encoding, Unicode, or polymorphism to bypass pattern-matching IDS rules.
  • Decoy Scanning and Spoofing: When running an Nmap scan, the -D flag adds decoy IP addresses. The target's IDS will see traffic coming from the attacker's real IP mixed with traffic from several spoofed IPs, making it extremely difficult to identify the true source of the attack.
  • Session Splicing and TCP Chaffing: Session splicing sends the payload across multiple TCP sessions extremely slowly, staying below IDS time thresholds. TCP Chaffing involves injecting garbage packets with bad checksums or modified TTLs. The IDS might process these packets, while the target OS drops them, causing a desynchronization in state between the IDS and the target.
  • Firewalking: An advanced technique used to determine the ACLs of a firewall. It works similarly to traceroute, incrementing the Time-to-Live (TTL) field. If a packet is dropped silently, the port is blocked. If an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message is returned by the router immediately past the firewall, the port is open and the packet passed through.

Honeypots

Honeypots are categorized into low-interaction and high-interaction. Low-interaction honeypots simply simulate services (like a fake SSH banner) to log connection attempts. High-interaction honeypots provide a full, vulnerable OS for the attacker to exploit, capturing advanced TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures). Attackers evade honeypots by checking for virtualization artifacts (MAC addresses, specific drivers) or analyzing unusual latency in network responses.

modules.title: IoT and OT Hacking
modules.description: Exhaustive breakdown of Internet of Things (IoT) protocols, Operational Technology (OT) and SCADA systems, industrial control security vulnerabilities, and cloud computing architectures.
objectives: Deconstruct IoT protocols like MQTT, CoAP, and Zigbee and their security implementations.
Analyze the Purdue Model and structural vulnerabilities in SCADA/ICS environments.
Exploit ICS protocols lacking authentication, such as Modbus TCP.
Evaluate core Cloud Computing concepts, service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), and deployment models.

topics.title: IoT Protocols and Architecture
topics.content:
IoT Architecture Layers

The Internet of Things (IoT) architecture typically consists of several layers: the Edge/Perception Layer (sensors, actuators, and physical devices), the Network/Transport Layer (gateways and communication protocols), and the Application/Cloud Layer (data processing, storage, and user interfaces). Security must be addressed at each layer.

Key IoT Protocols

Due to the constrained nature of IoT devices (low power, limited processing and memory), standard internet protocols are often too heavy. Specialized protocols are used:

  • MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport): A lightweight publish-subscribe messaging protocol. Devices (clients) publish data to a central broker under specific 'topics'. Other clients subscribe to these topics to receive data. A major security flaw occurs when brokers are configured without authentication, allowing anyone to subscribe to the wildcard topic (#) and intercept all telemetry data or publish malicious commands.
  • CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol): A specialized web transfer protocol for use with constrained nodes and networks. It translates HTTP-like semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to a lightweight UDP-based protocol. CoAP can be secured using DTLS (Datagram TLS).
  • Zigbee: An IEEE 802.15.4-based wireless protocol used for low-power, short-range mesh networking. Common in smart home automation (Phillips Hue, smart locks). Zigbee relies on a network key for encryption; if this key is sniffed during the initial pairing process, the network is compromised.
  • Z-Wave: Another low-energy wireless protocol primarily for home automation. It operates in the sub-1GHz band, avoiding 2.4 GHz interference.

topics.title: IoT Attack Surface and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
The IoT Attack Surface

IoT devices present a massive attack surface spanning hardware, network, and cloud. Many IoT devices are rushed to market with zero consideration for security, leading to fundamental flaws.

  • Default and Hardcoded Credentials: The single most exploited vulnerability in IoT. Devices often ship with default usernames and passwords (e.g., admin/admin, root/12345) that users are never forced to change. Some devices even have credentials hardcoded directly into the firmware. The infamous Mirai Botnet exploited exactly this, taking over millions of IP cameras and DVRs to launch massive DDoS attacks.
  • Insecure Firmware Updates: Many IoT devices pull firmware updates over unencrypted HTTP and fail to verify the digital signature of the update package. An attacker in a Man-in-the-Middle position can intercept the update request and feed the device a maliciously modified firmware image, permanently backdooring the device.
  • Hardware Exploitation: Attackers with physical access can interface directly with the device's hardware. By connecting to exposed UART, JTAG, or SPI interfaces on the circuit board, attackers can interrupt the boot process, drop into a root shell, or dump the firmware directly from the flash memory chip for reverse engineering using tools like binwalk.
  • Lack of Encryption: Telemetry data, video streams, and command traffic are often transmitted in plaintext. Without mutual TLS authentication, devices are susceptible to spoofing and traffic interception.

topics.title: OT/SCADA Security and Protocols
topics.content:
Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and SCADA

Operational Technology (OT) manages the physical processes in critical infrastructure—manufacturing lines, power grids, water treatment plants. SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a subset of ICS used to control geographically dispersed assets. A typical SCADA environment includes:

  • Sensors and Actuators: Devices that interact directly with physical processes.
  • PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) / RTU (Remote Terminal Unit): Ruggedized computers that read sensor data and execute control logic.
  • HMI (Human-Machine Interface): Graphical dashboards used by operators to monitor and control the PLCs.

The Purdue Model

The Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture is the standard for securing ICS environments through network segmentation. It separates the enterprise IT network (Levels 4 and 5) from the manufacturing/OT network (Levels 0 to 3) using industrial firewalls and DMZs (Level 3.5). This segmentation is critical to prevent a ransomware infection in the IT network from spreading to the PLCs and shutting down physical production.

OT Security Challenges and Protocols

While IT prioritizes Confidentiality (the CIA triad), OT prioritizes Availability and Safety above all else. Security controls cannot introduce latency or cause a process to halt unexpectedly.

Modbus TCP: One of the most prevalent industrial protocols. Designed in 1979, Modbus TCP completely lacks authentication, authorization, or encryption. Any device that can route packets to the PLC can read memory registers or write commands to alter physical processes. Attackers exploit this lack of security to manipulate valves or motors. Stuxnet, the most famous ICS malware, successfully sabotaged Iranian nuclear centrifuges by silently altering PLC logic while reporting normal operating conditions to the HMI.

topics.title: Cloud Computing Concepts
topics.content:
Cloud Service and Deployment Models

Cloud computing has revolutionized IT infrastructure, but it shifts the security paradigm. Understanding the models is fundamental.

  • IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service): The provider supplies raw computing resources (virtual machines, networking, storage). Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs. The customer is responsible for managing the OS, patching, and application security.
  • PaaS (Platform as a Service): The provider supplies the runtime environment, OS, and databases, allowing developers to focus purely on application code. Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku.
  • SaaS (Software as a Service): Fully managed applications delivered over the internet. Examples: Salesforce, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365. The customer only manages user access and data configurations.

Deployment models include Public Cloud (shared multi-tenant infrastructure), Private Cloud (dedicated infrastructure for a single organization), and Hybrid Cloud (a mix of both, often connecting an on-premises data center to a public cloud via a dedicated link or VPN).

The Shared Responsibility Model

Security in the cloud is shared between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer. The CSP is responsible for 'Security of the Cloud' (physical data centers, hardware, hypervisors). The customer is responsible for 'Security in the Cloud' (OS patching, IAM policies, firewall rules, and data encryption). A massive percentage of cloud breaches occur because customers misunderstand this model and assume the CSP handles everything, leading to misconfigured S3 buckets and overly permissive IAM roles.

modules.title: Cloud Computing and Applied Cryptography
modules.description: Deep dive into Cloud Security Posture Management, Serverless and Container security, alongside an exhaustive curriculum on fundamental and applied Cryptography.
objectives: Identify and exploit cloud misconfigurations, specifically focusing on IAM and exposed storage.
Analyze the security models of Containerization (Docker/Kubernetes) and Serverless architectures.
Master the mechanics of Symmetric (AES, DES) and Asymmetric (RSA, ECC) encryption algorithms.
Evaluate Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), digital signatures, hashing algorithms, and advanced cryptanalysis techniques.

topics.title: Cloud Security and IAM Misconfigurations
topics.content:
Identity and Access Management (IAM)

In cloud environments, the traditional network perimeter is dissolved; identity becomes the new security perimeter. IAM controls who (users, roles, or services) can access which resources under what conditions. Misconfigurations in IAM are the leading cause of cloud privilege escalation.

Assigning a policy like {"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "*", "Resource": "*"} grants total administrative control. If an attacker compromises a user or an EC2 instance with this role attached, they can take over the entire AWS account. The principle of least privilege must be rigorously applied.

SSRF and the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS)

A critical attack vector in AWS, Azure, and GCP involves Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). Cloud instances have a local, non-routable metadata service accessible at 169.254.169.254. If a web application running on an EC2 instance is vulnerable to SSRF, an attacker can force the application to make an HTTP request to this internal IP. By navigating to the metadata endpoint (e.g., latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/), the attacker can extract the temporary IAM access keys assigned to that instance. With these keys, they can access the cloud environment via the AWS CLI from their own machine. AWS introduced IMDSv2, which requires a session token, to mitigate this specific SSRF vector.

Cloud Storage Exposures

Misconfigured cloud storage, such as Amazon S3 buckets or Azure Blob Storage, leads to massive data breaches. If an administrator mistakenly sets the Access Control List (ACL) or bucket policy to 'public-read' or grants the 'All Users' group access, anyone on the internet can enumerate and download the contents of the bucket. Attackers use automated tools to scan for open buckets containing backups, PII, and source code.

topics.title: Container Security, Serverless, and Zero Trust
topics.content:
Container Security and Escapes

Containers (like Docker) package applications and their dependencies into isolated environments. Unlike VMs, containers share the host's operating system kernel. A Container Escape occurs when an attacker exploits a vulnerability (such as a kernel exploit or misconfiguration) to break out of the container's isolation and gain access to the underlying host node. Running containers in --privileged mode or mounting the Docker socket (/var/run/docker.sock) inside the container are severe misconfigurations that make escape trivial.

Kubernetes Orchestration Security

Kubernetes (K8s) is the industry standard for container orchestration. Security must be managed across the API server, kubelet, and pods. Misconfigurations such as allowing anonymous access to the Kubelet API, running pods with hostNetwork: true, or failing to implement Network Policies (which restrict lateral movement between pods) are common targets. A compromised pod can be used to query the Kubernetes API and pivot throughout the cluster.

Serverless Computing (FaaS) Risks

Function as a Service (e.g., AWS Lambda) removes infrastructure management entirely. The cloud provider handles the OS and hardware. While this eliminates host-level threats (like unpatched kernels), it shifts the attack surface entirely to the application layer. Insecure application code (injection flaws, insecure deserialization) remains the primary risk. Furthermore, over-privileged IAM execution roles assigned to serverless functions can lead to privilege escalation if the function is exploited.

The Zero Trust Model

Zero Trust is a security paradigm summarized as "Never trust, always verify." It assumes that the network is always hostile and that threats exist both inside and outside the perimeter. Access is granted continuously based on identity, device posture, and context, regardless of network location. It relies heavily on micro-segmentation and continuous authentication.

topics.title: Cryptography Fundamentals
topics.content:
Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption

Cryptography ensures Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, and Non-repudiation.

Symmetric Encryption: Uses a single, shared secret key to both encrypt and decrypt data. It is extremely fast and efficient, making it ideal for encrypting large volumes of data (e.g., full disk encryption, database encryption). However, it suffers from the key distribution problem—how to securely share the secret key over an insecure channel.

  • DES (Data Encryption Standard): Obsolete. Uses a 56-bit key which is trivial to brute-force today.
  • 3DES (Triple DES): Applies the DES algorithm three times to increase the key size, but is slow and largely deprecated.
  • AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): The modern gold standard. It uses a 128-bit block size and supports 128, 192, or 256-bit keys. Used worldwide for securing sensitive data.
  • Stream vs. Block Ciphers: Block ciphers (AES) encrypt data in fixed-size blocks. Stream ciphers (RC4) encrypt data bit-by-bit or byte-by-byte continuously.

Asymmetric Encryption: Uses a mathematically linked key pair—a Public Key (shared with everyone) and a Private Key (kept secret). It solves the key distribution problem. Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted by the corresponding private key. It is computationally heavy and much slower than symmetric encryption, so it is primarily used for secure key exchange and digital signatures.

  • RSA: Security relies on the mathematical difficulty of factoring large prime numbers.
  • ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography): Provides the same level of security as RSA but with significantly smaller key sizes, resulting in faster processing and lower power consumption. Excellent for mobile and IoT devices.
  • Diffie-Hellman (DH): A key exchange protocol that allows two parties to securely establish a shared symmetric key over an insecure channel without transmitting the key itself.

topics.title: Hashing, PKI, and Advanced Cryptography
topics.content:
Hashing and Integrity

A hash function takes an input of any size and produces a fixed-size string of characters (a digest). It is a one-way function—it cannot be reversed. Hashes are used to verify data integrity; even a single bit change in the input produces a completely different hash (the Avalanche Effect). Common algorithms include MD5 (broken, produces collisions), SHA-1 (deprecated), and SHA-256/SHA-3.

Password Hashing and Salting: Passwords should never be stored in plaintext. They must be hashed. To prevent attackers from using Rainbow Tables (precomputed hash chains), a unique random value called a Salt is appended to the password before hashing. Modern systems use slow hashing algorithms like bcrypt or PBKDF2 to thwart brute-force attacks.

Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

PKI is the framework that manages digital certificates and public-key encryption. At its core is the Certificate Authority (CA), a trusted third party that issues and signs digital certificates, binding a public key to an entity's identity (like a website domain). When you visit an HTTPS website, your browser verifies the server's certificate against its pre-installed list of trusted Root CAs.

Digital Signatures: Provide authentication, non-repudiation, and integrity. A sender hashes a document and encrypts the hash with their Private Key. The receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's Public Key to verify it. If it decrypts successfully, it proves the sender created it and the document wasn't altered.

Advanced Concepts

  • Homomorphic Encryption: A cutting-edge concept that allows mathematical operations to be performed directly on ciphertext. The result, when decrypted, matches the result of the operations as if they had been performed on the plaintext. This allows cloud providers to process sensitive data without ever seeing the plaintext.
  • Quantum Computing Threat: Shor's algorithm, executable on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer, can easily factor large primes, effectively breaking RSA and ECC. NIST is currently standardizing Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) algorithms, utilizing lattice-based mathematics (like ML-KEM/Kyber) to resist quantum attacks.

topics.title: Cryptanalysis and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Cryptanalysis Attack Models

Cryptanalysis is the study of analyzing and breaking cryptographic systems. Attacks are categorized by the information available to the attacker:

  • Ciphertext-only Attack: The attacker only has access to a set of ciphertexts. This is the hardest attack model.
  • Known-plaintext Attack: The attacker has access to the ciphertext and the corresponding plaintext. This helps deduce the key.
  • Chosen-plaintext Attack: The attacker can choose arbitrary plaintexts to be encrypted and obtain the corresponding ciphertexts.
  • Chosen-ciphertext Attack: The attacker can choose different ciphertexts and obtain the decrypted plaintexts.

Specific Cryptographic Attacks

  • Birthday Attack: A statistical attack used to find collisions in hash functions. It is based on the birthday paradox, showing that the probability of two inputs producing the same hash is much higher than intuition suggests. It effectively halves the bit strength of a hash function against collision attacks.
  • Meet-in-the-Middle Attack: Specifically targets Double DES (2DES). The attacker encrypts the plaintext with all possible first keys and decrypts the ciphertext with all possible second keys, storing the intermediate results in tables. If a match is found in the middle, the keys are discovered. This is why 2DES is not used, and 3DES was required.
  • POODLE Attack: Exploited a vulnerability in the obsolete SSL 3.0 protocol, allowing an MitM attacker to decrypt secure HTTP cookies. It forced the industry to completely disable SSL 3.0.
  • Heartbleed: A catastrophic vulnerability in the OpenSSL library's TLS heartbeat extension. It allowed attackers to read up to 64KB of server memory in plaintext, potentially exposing private SSL keys, usernames, and passwords.

Extract and reverse-engineer IoT and router firmware to discover zero-day vulnerabilities.

topics.title: IoT Architecture and the Edge Layer
topics.content:
Edge Computing in IoT

As IoT deployments scale, sending all raw sensor data directly to the central cloud for processing causes significant latency and bandwidth bottlenecks. Edge Computing pushes data processing, analytics, and intelligence closer to the source—at the 'edge' of the network, typically on IoT gateways or high-powered edge devices.

From a security perspective, edge computing changes the threat model. Instead of securing 'dumb' sensors that just transmit data, security teams must now protect powerful edge nodes running complex operating systems, local databases, and machine learning models. If an edge node is compromised, the attacker gains a highly capable pivot point within the local network, capable of aggregating sensitive data or orchestrating attacks against connected local sensors without ever traversing the internet firewall.

Secure Boot and Hardware Root of Trust

To secure edge devices and advanced IoT hardware, physical security and boot integrity are paramount. A Hardware Root of Trust involves a dedicated cryptographic chip (like a TPM - Trusted Platform Module) that securely stores cryptographic keys and performs hardware-based attestation.

Secure Boot ensures that a device only boots software that is trusted by the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM). When the device turns on, the bootloader verifies the digital signature of the OS kernel using a public key burned into the hardware. If an attacker flashes a malicious firmware image, the signature validation fails, and the device refuses to boot, preventing persistent hardware backdoors.

topics.title: Advanced IoT and Wireless Threats
topics.content:
Advanced Wireless Attacks

Beyond standard WPA2 cracking, advanced attackers target enterprise wireless infrastructure and specialized protocols.

  • EAP-TLS Downgrade Attacks: Enterprise networks use 802.1X/EAP for authentication. EAP-TLS is the most secure method, requiring client certificates. Attackers may attempt to downgrade the authentication request to a weaker protocol like PEAP-MSCHAPv2 by setting up a rogue RADIUS server. If the client device doesn't strictly enforce certificate validation, it may accept the downgrade and transmit its MSCHAPv2 challenge-response, which the attacker captures and cracks offline.
  • SDR (Software Defined Radio) Replay Attacks: Many IoT devices, car key fobs, and garage door openers operate on sub-GHz frequencies (e.g., 315 MHz, 433 MHz). Attackers use SDR hardware (like the HackRF or RTL-SDR) to record the radio frequency transmissions. In a standard replay attack, the attacker simply re-transmits the recorded signal to unlock the door. To defeat rolling codes (which change every time the button is pressed), attackers use techniques like RollJam, which jams the legitimate signal while simultaneously recording it, ensuring the attacker holds the next valid, unused code.

Side-Channel Attacks on Hardware

Attackers with physical access to a device don't always need a software vulnerability. Side-Channel Attacks extract secret keys by monitoring the physical emissions of the device during cryptographic operations. Examples include Power Analysis (measuring minute fluctuations in power consumption while the CPU encrypts data) and Electromagnetic (EM) Analysis. These attacks can extract AES or RSA keys directly from the hardware, bypassing all software protections.

topics.title: OT/ICS Systems and Cyber-Physical Threats
topics.content:
Cyber-Physical Impacts

The defining characteristic of hacking OT/ICS systems is the translation of digital commands into kinetic, physical consequences. Unlike IT breaches where data is stolen, OT breaches result in power outages, environmental disasters, or loss of human life.

  • Triton/Trisis Malware: While Stuxnet targeted the process control PLCs, the Triton malware discovered in 2017 represented a massive escalation: it targeted the Safety Instrumented System (SIS). The SIS is the fail-safe mechanism designed to shut down the plant safely if the primary control system fails or enters a dangerous state. By compromising the safety controllers, the attackers disabled the plant's ability to protect itself, opening the door for a catastrophic industrial accident.
  • Industroyer/CrashOverride: Malware specifically designed to attack power grids, utilized in the 2016 Ukraine power grid cyberattack. It directly implemented industrial communication protocols (IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850) to speak natively to electrical substation switches and circuit breakers, forcing them open and causing massive blackouts.

ICS Vulnerability Discovery

Discovering vulnerabilities in ICS equipment is complex due to the proprietary nature of the hardware and protocols. Security researchers rely heavily on Protocol Fuzzing. By sending massive amounts of malformed, unexpected, or random data to a PLC over protocols like DNP3, CIP, or Profinet, researchers monitor the device for crashes or unexpected behavior. Because PLCs are designed for stability, they often lack robust input validation and are highly susceptible to denial-of-service conditions when fuzzed, causing physical processes to halt.

topics.title: ICS Network Architecture and Firmware Analysis
topics.content:
Advanced Network Segmentation

Securing ICS environments demands rigorous adherence to the Purdue Model, moving beyond simple VLANs. Modern architectures employ Unidirectional Gateways (Data Diodes) between the OT network and the IT network. A data diode is a physical hardware device that allows data to flow in only one direction. For example, it allows historian data and sensor readings to flow out of the OT network to the corporate network for analysis, but physically prevents any packets from flowing in from the IT network. This completely nullifies external network-based attacks attempting to reach the control systems.

Firmware Reverse Engineering

Analyzing IoT and router firmware is a crucial skill for discovering zero-day vulnerabilities in embedded systems.

  1. Acquisition: Firmware is acquired by downloading it from the vendor's support site, intercepting an OTA (Over-The-Air) update, or dumping it directly from the hardware flash chip using a bus pirate or JTAG interface.
  2. Extraction: The binwalk tool is the industry standard for analyzing binary files. It scans the firmware image for known file signatures and can extract compressed file systems like SquashFS, JFFS2, or CramFS.
  3. Analysis: Once the file system is extracted, researchers have access to the embedded Linux environment. They examine startup scripts (e.g., /etc/init.d) to see what services run on boot. They search for hardcoded credentials in configuration files, exposed private SSH keys, and backdoor accounts.
  4. Binary Exploitation: Finally, researchers reverse-engineer the proprietary binaries (like the web server handling the administrative interface) using disassemblers like Ghidra or IDA Pro to find buffer overflows or command injection vulnerabilities.
